title: Physics
description: Physics from scratch for complete beginners: 15 sub-topics from measurement and motion through forces, energy, waves, heat, electricity, light and a peek at modern physics. Beginner-first readings, a diagram in nearly every lesson, MCQ practice, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: What Physics Is & How Science Works
lessons.content:
Have you ever wondered why a dropped ball falls down, why the sky is blue, or how a phone screen glows? **Physics** is the study of how the world works at its most basic level: matter (the stuff things are made of), energy (the ability to make things happen, like heat or motion), motion, and forces (pushes and pulls). Almost everything you see is physics in action.

Physics does not work by guessing or by trusting authority. It works by a careful routine called the **scientific method**. Think of it like a detective solving a case. First you **observe** something interesting (for example, ice melts in the sun). Then you make a **hypothesis**, which is a testable guess about why (perhaps sunlight adds energy that melts ice). From that guess you make a **prediction** (ice in shade should melt slower). Then you **test** it with an experiment and compare what happens to what you predicted. If the result disagrees, you **refine** the idea and go around the loop again.

A key word is **model**: a simplified picture of reality that is good enough to make useful predictions, even if it leaves out details. A map is a model of a city. Physics is full of models.

In short: physics studies matter, energy, motion, and forces, and it uses the observe-hypothesize-predict-test-refine cycle to build models we can trust. Next we look at how scientists describe those things with numbers.

items.prompt: Which group best describes the things physics studies at the most basic level?
choices: Grammar, history, music, and art
Matter, energy, motion, and forces
Money, laws, and elections
Feelings, opinions, and tastes
items.explanation: Physics is the study of matter (the stuff things are made of), energy, motion, and forces (pushes and pulls). The other lists belong to other fields entirely; physics deals only with things you can measure about the physical world.

items.prompt: Put the steps of the scientific method in their correct order.
choices: Predict, observe, test, hypothesize
Test, refine, observe, hypothesize
Observe, hypothesize, predict, test
Hypothesize, observe, predict, refine
items.explanation: You first observe something interesting, then form a hypothesis (a testable guess about why), then make a prediction of what should follow, then test it with an experiment. Starting with a prediction or a test before observing (choices 1 and 2) puts the cart before the horse.

items.prompt: What makes a guess count as a scientific 'hypothesis' rather than just an opinion?
choices: It must be testable - you can check it against an experiment or observation
It must come from a famous scientist
It must already be proven true
It must use complicated words
items.explanation: A hypothesis is a testable guess about why something happens; the key feature is that you can check it against evidence. Whether it comes from an expert, or sounds impressive, is irrelevant - if there is no way to test it, it is not a scientific hypothesis.

items.prompt: A subway map leaves out real street distances but still gets you from station to station. In science, this is an example of a:
choices: Hypothesis
Model
Measurement
Prediction
items.explanation: A model is a simplified picture of reality that drops unimportant details yet still makes useful predictions - exactly like a subway map. It is not a hypothesis (a guess to test) or a prediction (a stated outcome); its job is to be 'good enough' to be useful.

items.prompt: A scientist notices plants near a window grow taller, guesses light causes it, and says 'plants in the dark should grow shorter.' What is that statement?
choices: An observation
A hypothesis
A prediction
A conclusion
items.explanation: A prediction states what should happen IF the guess is true, so you can test it. The observation was 'plants near the window grow taller'; the hypothesis was 'light causes it'; the prediction is the testable 'plants in the dark should grow shorter.' Naming each step keeps the reasoning honest.

items.prompt: An experiment's result disagrees with the prediction. According to the scientific method, what should happen next?
choices: Ignore the result and keep the hypothesis
Refine or replace the hypothesis and test again
Declare the experiment broken
Stop doing science on the topic
items.explanation: A disagreement is information, not failure. The method loops: you adjust the idea to fit the evidence and test the new version. Ignoring data (choice 1) or blaming the experiment without checking (choice 3) would defeat the whole point of testing.

items.prompt: Why do physicists use 'models' (simplified pictures of reality)?
choices: Because reality is too boring to study directly
Because a simplified picture can still make useful, accurate predictions
Because models are always perfectly complete
Because guessing is faster than measuring
items.explanation: A model deliberately drops details that do not matter for the question at hand, making the problem solvable while still predicting well. It is not claimed to be complete (choice 2); its value is being 'good enough,' like a subway map that ignores real street distances.

items.prompt: Which of these is the best example of a physics question?
choices: Which painting is the most beautiful?
How fast will a ball roll down this ramp?
What is the kindest thing to say to a friend?
Which song is the catchiest?
items.explanation: Physics deals with matter, energy, motion, and forces, and asks questions you can measure and test. 'How fast will a ball roll' has a measurable, testable answer. The others are matters of taste or ethics, which science cannot settle with an experiment.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation is the clearest example of 'physics in action'?
choices: Choosing your favorite color
A dropped ball falling to the ground
Deciding which movie is best
Picking a name for a pet
items.explanation: Physics studies matter, energy, motion, and forces. A falling ball involves a force (gravity) producing motion - measurable, testable physics. The other choices are personal preferences, which have no measurable physical answer.

lessons.title: Physical Quantities & SI Units
lessons.content:
Imagine telling a friend a recipe needs 'some flour for a while.' Useless, right? You need a number and a unit: '200 grams for 30 minutes.' Physics is the same. A **physical quantity** is anything you can measure, and stating it always needs two parts: a **number** (how many) and a **unit** (of what). Saying a table is '2' means nothing; '2 meters' means something.

To stop confusion, scientists worldwide agreed on one shared set called the **SI units** (from the French *Systeme International*, 'International System'). At its heart are **7 base units**, the building blocks from which all others are made:

- length -> meter (m)
- mass (how much matter) -> kilogram (kg)
- time -> second (s)
- electric current -> ampere (A)
- temperature -> kelvin (K)
- amount of substance -> mole (mol)
- luminous intensity (brightness) -> candela (cd)

Every other unit is built by combining these. Speed, for example, is length divided by time, measured in meters per second (m/s). These combinations are called **derived units**.

One caution: mass and weight are not the same. **Mass** is how much matter is in an object (kilograms) and never changes; weight is the pull of gravity on that mass and would be smaller on the Moon.

The takeaway: every measurement is a number plus a unit, and the SI system gives the whole world 7 base units to build everything else from.

items.prompt: A friend says 'the rope is 4.' Why is this not a complete measurement?
choices: It is missing a unit, so '4' does not say 4 of what
It is missing a number
Ropes cannot be measured
The number 4 is too small to be useful
items.explanation: Every measurement needs two parts: a number (how many) and a unit (of what). '4' gives the number but no unit, so we cannot tell if it means 4 meters, 4 centimeters, or 4 feet. A number alone is meaningless.

items.prompt: What does 'SI' stand for?
choices: Scientific Index
Standard Imperial
Systeme International (International System)
Simple Identification
items.explanation: SI comes from the French Systeme International, meaning 'International System' of units. It is the single shared set of units agreed on by scientists worldwide so everyone measures the same way. It is deliberately not the imperial (inches and pounds) system.

items.prompt: What is the SI base unit of mass, and its symbol?
choices: gram (g)
kilogram (kg)
newton (N)
pound (lb)
items.explanation: The SI base unit of mass is the kilogram (kg) - unusual because its name already contains the prefix 'kilo.' The gram is smaller and not the base unit; the newton measures force (weight), not mass; the pound is an imperial unit.

items.prompt: How many SI base units are there?
items.explanation: There are exactly 7 base units (meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela). They are special because every other SI unit is built by combining them, so this small set anchors all of physics.

items.prompt: Which list correctly pairs an SI base quantity with its base unit?
choices: time -> meter
temperature -> kelvin
mass -> second
length -> ampere
items.explanation: Temperature is measured in kelvin (K). The others are scrambled: time uses the second, mass uses the kilogram, and length uses the meter. The ampere is for electric current. Memorizing the seven correct pairings is the foundation for every other unit.

items.prompt: Speed is measured in meters per second (m/s). A unit like this, built by combining base units, is called a:
choices: Base unit
Derived unit
Conversion factor
Prefix
items.explanation: Speed is length divided by time, so its unit (m/s) is built from the base units meter and second - that combination is a derived unit. A base unit stands alone (like the meter); m/s is made from two base units, so it is derived.

items.prompt: An astronaut who weighs less on the Moon than on Earth has, on the Moon:
choices: Less mass and less weight
The same mass but less weight
More mass but the same weight
The same mass and the same weight
items.explanation: Mass (the amount of matter) does not change with location, so it stays the same. Weight is gravity's pull, and the Moon's gravity is weaker, so weight drops. Confusing the two is one of the most common beginner mistakes.

items.prompt: Which statement about mass and weight is correct?
choices: Mass is the pull of gravity; weight is the amount of matter
Mass never changes with location; weight depends on gravity
Mass and weight are just two words for the same thing
Weight stays the same everywhere; mass changes on the Moon
items.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object (in kilograms) and is the same everywhere. Weight is the force of gravity pulling on that mass, so it changes with location - less on the Moon. Choice 1 swaps the two definitions; choice 4 reverses which one depends on gravity.

items.prompt: Why did the world's scientists agree to use the SI system?
choices: To make physics harder for students
So measurements mean the same thing everywhere and results can be shared
Because the metric system was invented first
To replace all numbers with symbols
items.explanation: A shared standard means a '5-kilogram' object is the same in Tokyo, Lagos, or New York, so experiments can be compared and trusted worldwide. Without a common system, every lab would speak a different measurement 'language.'

lessons.title: Scientific Notation, Powers of Ten & Estimation
lessons.content:
Physics deals with the unbelievably tiny (an atom is about 0.0000000001 meters wide) and the unbelievably huge (a galaxy spans trillions of kilometers). Writing out all those zeros is painful and error-prone. **Scientific notation** is a shortcut for writing very big or very small numbers neatly.

The idea rests on **powers of ten**. The little raised number, called the **exponent**, tells you how many times to multiply or divide by 10. So $10^3$ means $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$ (a 1 with three zeros). A negative exponent means dividing: $10^{-3}$ means $1 \div 1000 = 0.001$.

In scientific notation you write a number as a value between 1 and 10, multiplied by a power of ten. For example, 4500 becomes $4.5 \times 10^3$, and 0.0072 becomes $7.2 \times 10^{-3}$. The exponent just counts how many places the decimal point moved: right for big numbers (positive), left for small (negative).

A related tool is the **order of magnitude**: the nearest power of ten to a quantity, used to judge rough size. A house and a person differ by about one order of magnitude in height (roughly 10 times). Quick rough calculations using these are called **estimation** or **Fermi estimates** - useful for sanity-checking an answer.

Takeaway: powers of ten let us write any size cleanly, and orders of magnitude let us reason about scale fast.

items.prompt: In the power of ten $10^3$, what does the exponent 3 tell you to do?
choices: Add 3 to 10
Multiply 10 by itself 3 times: 10 x 10 x 10 = 1000
Divide 10 by 3
Multiply 10 by 3 to get 30
items.explanation: A positive exponent tells you how many times to multiply by 10. So $10^3$ = 10 x 10 x 10 = 1000 (a 1 with three zeros). It is repeated multiplication, not adding 3 (which gives 13) or multiplying by 3 (which gives 30).

items.prompt: How is 1000 written as a power of ten?
items.explanation: 1000 is a 1 followed by three zeros, which is 10 x 10 x 10 = $10^3$. A quick rule: the exponent equals the number of zeros. $10^2$ is only 100, and $10^4$ is 10000.

items.prompt: What does $10^{-2}$ equal?
items.explanation: A negative exponent means dividing by 10 that many times: 1 divided by 10 divided by 10 = 0.01. A negative exponent makes a number small and positive; it never makes the number itself negative, so choices 1 and 2 are traps.

items.prompt: Which number is written correctly in proper scientific notation?
items.explanation: Scientific notation is a value between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of ten, so $4.5 \times 10^3$ is correct. $45 \times 10^2$ and $0.45 \times 10^4$ equal the same size but break the 'between 1 and 10' rule, and choice 4 uses addition instead of multiplication.

items.prompt: The number 60,000 written in scientific notation is:
items.explanation: Move the decimal point left until one nonzero digit remains before it: 60000 becomes 6, and the point moved 4 places, giving $6 \times 10^4$. Choice 3 ($60 \times 10^3$) equals the right value but is not proper scientific notation, because the leading part must be between 1 and 10.

items.prompt: The small number 0.0072 written in scientific notation is:
items.explanation: For a number smaller than 1, move the decimal point right until one nonzero digit sits in front: 0.0072 becomes 7.2, and the point moved 3 places right, so the exponent is -3, giving $7.2 \times 10^{-3}$. The negative sign signals a number less than 1, not a negative value.

items.prompt: What is an 'order of magnitude' difference between two quantities?
choices: A difference of about 2 times
A difference of about 10 times (one power of ten)
A difference of exactly 100
Any difference at all
items.explanation: An order of magnitude is the nearest power of ten, so two things one order of magnitude apart differ by roughly 10 times. A house is about one order of magnitude taller than a person. Two orders would be about 100 times, not one.

items.prompt: An atom is about $10^{-10}$ m wide and a person is about $10^0$ m tall. About how many orders of magnitude separate them?
items.explanation: Subtract the exponents: 0 minus (-10) = 10 orders of magnitude. Each order is a factor of 10, so a person is roughly ten-billion times larger than an atom. Counting steps on a powers-of-ten number line is the fast way to compare wildly different sizes.

items.prompt: A quick, rough calculation using powers of ten and reasonable guesses to get an answer's approximate size is called a:
choices: Fermi estimate
Exact proof
Conversion factor
Significant figure
items.explanation: A Fermi estimate (named after physicist Enrico Fermi) uses round numbers and powers of ten to get a ballpark answer fast - great for sanity-checking before exact math. It is the opposite of an exact proof, and unrelated to conversion factors or significant figures.

items.prompt: Why is scientific notation useful in physics?
choices: It makes every number negative
It writes very large or very small numbers compactly and reduces zero-counting errors
It is only for money
It removes the need for units
items.explanation: Quantities in physics range from atom-sized to galaxy-sized. Writing $3 \times 10^8$ instead of 300000000 is shorter and far less error-prone. It does not change a number's sign or its units; it only changes how the number is written.

lessons.title: Unit Conversion, Precision vs Accuracy & Significant Figures
lessons.content:
In 1999 a Mars spacecraft was lost because one team used metric units and another used imperial ones. The lesson: converting units correctly matters. **Unit conversion** means rewriting a quantity in different units without changing its real size, by multiplying by a **conversion factor** - a fraction equal to 1, like $\frac{1000\,\text{m}}{1\,\text{km}}$. Because top and bottom are equal, multiplying by it does not change the value, only the units. So 3 km becomes $3 \times 1000 = 3000$ m. Lining up units so the unwanted one cancels is called **dimensional analysis**, and it catches mistakes fast.

Now, two words people mix up. **Accuracy** is how close a measurement is to the true value. **Precision** is how close repeated measurements are to each other. Picture darts on a board: tight darts far from the bullseye are precise but not accurate; scattered darts centered on the bullseye are accurate but not precise. You want both.

Finally, **significant figures** (sig figs) are the digits in a measurement that actually carry reliable information. If a ruler reads to the millimeter, writing 4.5 m pretends to less detail than 4.500 m does. A simple rule: your answer should not look more precise than your least precise measurement. So $2.0 \times 3.14159$ is reported as 6.3, not 6.28318.

Takeaway: convert units by canceling, keep accuracy and precision distinct, and let significant figures honestly reflect how good your measurement really was.

items.prompt: A conversion factor like 1000 m / 1 km has what value, and why does multiplying by it work?
choices: It equals 1000, so it makes numbers bigger
It equals 1, so it changes the units but not the real size
It equals 0, so it cancels everything
It equals the answer you want directly
items.explanation: Since 1000 m and 1 km are the same length, the fraction 1000 m / 1 km equals exactly 1. Multiplying any quantity by 1 leaves its real size unchanged - only the units are rewritten. That is the whole trick behind unit conversion.

items.prompt: Convert 5 kilometers (km) to meters (m), given 1 km = 1000 m.
items.explanation: Multiply by the conversion factor 1000 m / 1 km: 5 km x 1000 m/km = 5000 m. The 'km' units cancel, leaving meters. Since a meter is smaller than a kilometer, the number gets larger - a quick sanity check that you multiplied rather than divided.

items.prompt: Tracking units through a calculation so the unwanted units cancel and leave the units you want is called:
choices: Dimensional analysis
Scientific notation
Estimation
Rounding
items.explanation: Dimensional analysis means lining up conversion factors so units cancel like numbers, leaving the target unit. It is a fast way to catch setup mistakes - if your leftover units are wrong, your setup is wrong. It is not the same as scientific notation or rounding.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes accuracy from precision?
choices: Accuracy is how close repeated readings are to each other; precision is closeness to the true value
Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is how close repeated readings are to each other
They mean exactly the same thing
Accuracy applies to length; precision applies to time
items.explanation: Accuracy is how close a measurement is to the true value; precision is how close repeated measurements are to each other. Choice 1 swaps the two definitions - a very common mix-up. You can have one without the other, which is why both words exist.

items.prompt: A scale always reads 2 kg too high. Five weighings of the same object give nearly identical readings. These measurements are:
choices: Accurate but not precise
Precise but not accurate
Both accurate and precise
Neither accurate nor precise
items.explanation: The readings agree closely with each other, so they are precise. But a constant 2 kg offset pushes them all away from the true value, so they are not accurate. This is the 'tight cluster off the bullseye' dartboard - a classic sign of a systematic error.

items.prompt: Which dartboard pattern best represents 'accurate but not precise'?
choices: Darts tightly grouped on the bullseye
Darts tightly grouped far from the bullseye
Darts scattered widely but centered around the bullseye
Darts scattered widely and off to one side
items.explanation: Accuracy means centered on the true value (the bullseye); precision means tightly grouped. Scattered-but-centered darts average out to the truth (accurate) but disagree with one another (not precise). Tight-on-bullseye is both; tight-but-off is precise only.

items.prompt: What do 'significant figures' tell you about a measurement?
choices: How many digits carry reliable information about how precisely it was measured
Which digits are the largest in value
How many times the measurement was repeated
The number of units involved
items.explanation: Significant figures are the digits that actually carry reliable information, signaling how precisely something was measured. Writing 4.500 m claims more detail (and a finer instrument) than 4.5 m. They are about reliability of digits, not their size or how many units appear.

items.prompt: Using the standard rule, $2.0 \times 3.14159$ should be reported as:
items.explanation: The final answer should be no more precise than the least precise measurement. Here 2.0 has only 2 significant figures, so the result is rounded to 2 figures: 6.3. Reporting 6.28318 falsely claims precision the value 2.0 never had.

items.prompt: You measure a length as 3.0 m (2 significant figures) and another as 1.25 m. The standard rule says your final reported answer should:
choices: Have as many digits as the calculator shows
Be no more precise than the least precise measurement (here, 2 significant figures)
Always be a whole number
Drop all decimal points
items.explanation: An answer cannot be more reliable than the weakest measurement that went into it. Since 3.0 m has only 2 significant figures, the result should be rounded to 2 as well. Reporting every calculator digit (choice 1) falsely claims precision the measurement never had.

items.prompt: Why was the 1999 Mars spacecraft lost, and what lesson does it teach?
choices: The rocket ran out of fuel; always carry extra fuel
One team used metric units and another used imperial; converting units correctly matters
The math was too hard; avoid calculations
The crew misread a clock; ignore time measurements
items.explanation: The spacecraft was lost because one team worked in metric units while another used imperial, and the mismatch was never converted. The lesson is that careful unit conversion is not busywork - getting it wrong can wreck a real mission.

lessons.title: Position, Distance, and Displacement
lessons.content:
Imagine telling a friend where you are. You might say "I'm 200 meters past the bakery." That simple statement is the heart of describing motion. Before we can talk about how things move, we need a clear way to say *where* they are.

**Position** means where an object is, measured from an agreed starting point called the **origin** (the "zero" mark). Position needs a direction too: "200 meters east of the origin" is different from "200 meters west." We often draw this on a **number line**, where the origin is 0 and positions to one side are positive, the other side negative.

Now suppose you walk somewhere and come back. Two different ideas describe your trip. **Distance** is the total length of the path you actually walked, like the number on a fitness tracker. It is always positive and has no direction. **Displacement** is the straight-line change from start to finish, *including direction*: simply your ending position minus your starting position.

Here is the key surprise: they are often not equal. Walk 300 m east, then 200 m back west. Distance = 500 m (everything you walked). Displacement = 100 m east (where you ended up compared to the start). If you return exactly to where you began, your displacement is zero even though you walked a lot.

In short: position says where you are, distance says how far you travelled in total, and displacement says how far and in which direction you ended up from the start.

items.prompt: In physics, what does the 'origin' mean when describing position?
choices: The agreed starting point, the zero mark you measure positions from
The fastest point an object reaches
The place where an object finally stops
The total length of the path travelled
items.explanation: The origin is the reference 'zero' from which positions are measured; positions are stated relative to it. It is not about speed (that is unrelated) and not the stopping point (an object can be far from the origin when it stops). The total path length is distance, a different idea.

items.prompt: You walk 300 m east, then 200 m back west. What total distance did you travel?
items.explanation: Distance is the total path length, so you add every part you walked: 300 + 200 = 500 m. The answer 100 m is the displacement (the net change in position), not the distance, which is the common trap here.

items.prompt: For the same trip (300 m east, then 200 m back west), what is your displacement?
choices: 500 m east
100 m west
100 m east
0 m
items.explanation: Displacement is ending position minus starting position, with direction. You end up 100 m east of where you started, so it is 100 m east. It is not 500 m (that is total distance), and the direction is east, not west, because you are still east of the origin.

items.prompt: A runner does one full lap of a 400 m track and stops exactly where she started. What is her displacement?
choices: 0 m
items.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line change from start to finish. Since she ends exactly where she began, that change is zero. The 400 m is the distance (the path she covered); displacement and distance are not the same, which is exactly the point of this question.

items.prompt: Which statement is true about distance compared with displacement?
choices: Distance always has a direction; displacement never does
Distance is the total path length and is never negative; displacement includes direction
They are always equal for every trip
Displacement is always larger than distance
items.explanation: Distance is a total length (always zero or positive, no direction), while displacement is the net change in position and carries a direction. They are equal only for a straight, no-reversal trip. Displacement can never be larger than distance, so the last option is wrong, and direction belongs to displacement, not distance.

items.prompt: On a number line, an object moves from position +5 m to position -3 m. What is its displacement?
items.explanation: Displacement = final position minus initial position = (-3) - (+5) = -8 m. The negative sign tells you the direction (toward the negative side). Choosing +8 m forgets the direction; the magnitude is 8 m but the sign matters.

items.prompt: Why do we usually pick a positive direction (such as 'east is positive') before describing motion on a line?
choices: So we can express direction simply using plus and minus signs
Because objects can only move in the positive direction
To make every displacement come out positive
Because distance requires a chosen direction
items.explanation: Choosing a positive direction lets us write direction compactly: + for one way, - for the other. Objects can move either way (so the second choice is false), and displacements can be negative (so the third is false). Distance has no direction at all, making the last choice wrong.

items.prompt: A car drives 4 km north, then 3 km north again. How do its distance and displacement compare?
choices: Distance 7 km, displacement 1 km
Distance 7 km, displacement 7 km north
Distance 1 km, displacement 7 km
Distance 7 km, displacement 0 km
items.explanation: Because the car never reverses direction, the path length and the net change are the same: 7 km, and the displacement points north. Distance and displacement are equal only when motion is in a single straight direction with no turning back, which is the case here.

items.prompt: Which of these is a correct way to state a position?
choices: 60 kilometers per hour
12 meters to the right of the origin
5 seconds
30 meters total walked
items.explanation: A position tells where something is relative to the origin, with direction: '12 meters to the right of the origin' does exactly that. '60 km/h' is a speed, '5 seconds' is a time, and '30 meters total walked' is a distance, not a position.

lessons.title: Speed, Velocity, and Acceleration
lessons.content:
When something moves, we want to know not just *where* it is but *how fast* it is going and whether it is speeding up. Three closely related ideas cover this.

**Speed** is how much distance you cover each second, with no direction attached. Your car's speedometer shows speed. We compute it as distance divided by time. If you cover 100 m in 20 s, your speed is 100 / 20 = 5 meters per second, written 5 m/s (meters per second).

**Velocity** is speed *with a direction*: "5 m/s east." Formally it is displacement divided by time, $v=\\frac{\\Delta x}{\\Delta t}$, where the symbol $\\Delta$ (the Greek letter delta) means "change in," $\\Delta x$ is the change in position, and $\\Delta t$ is the time taken. Because velocity uses displacement, it can be negative (meaning the opposite direction). Two cars going the same speed in opposite directions have different velocities.

**Acceleration** is how quickly velocity changes each second. Press the gas and you accelerate; brake and you accelerate in the opposite sense (often called deceleration). We write $a=\\frac{\\Delta v}{\\Delta t}$, change in velocity over time. Its unit is meters per second, per second, written m/s$^2$. If a car goes from 0 to 10 m/s in 5 s, its acceleration is 10 / 5 = 2 m/s$^2$, meaning velocity grows by 2 m/s every second.

In short: speed is how fast (no direction), velocity is how fast *and which way*, and acceleration is how quickly the velocity itself is changing.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between speed and velocity?
choices: Velocity includes a direction; speed does not
Speed includes a direction; velocity does not
Speed is always larger than velocity
They are different names for acceleration
items.explanation: Velocity is speed together with a direction (e.g. '5 m/s east'); plain speed has no direction. Speed is not generally larger than velocity's size, and neither one is acceleration, which measures how velocity changes.

items.prompt: A cyclist covers 150 m in 30 s at a steady rate. What is her speed?
choices: 5 m/s
items.explanation: Speed = distance / time = 150 / 30 = 5 m/s. Dividing the other way (30 / 150) gives the wrong 0.2 m/s, and multiplying (150 x 30) gives the nonsensical 4500. Always divide distance by time for speed.

items.prompt: In the formula $v=\\frac{\\Delta x}{\\Delta t}$, what does the symbol $\\Delta$ (delta) mean?
choices: 'Change in'
'Multiply by'
'Square root of'
'Average of'
items.explanation: The Greek letter delta is shorthand for 'change in', so $\\Delta x$ is the change in position and $\\Delta t$ the change in time. It does not mean multiply, square root, or average; those are different operations.

items.prompt: What does acceleration measure?
choices: How far an object has travelled
How fast the velocity is changing each second
The total distance divided by total time
The object's exact position
items.explanation: Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes, found from change in velocity over time. Total distance is just distance, distance over time is average speed, and position is where the object is, none of which is acceleration.

items.prompt: A car speeds up from 0 to 12 m/s in 4 s. What is its acceleration?
items.explanation: Acceleration = change in velocity / time = (12 - 0) / 4 = 3 m/s$^2$, meaning velocity rises by 3 m/s each second. Multiplying (12 x 4 = 48) or dividing the wrong way (4 / 12) gives the wrong answers; adding gives 16.

items.prompt: Why is the unit of acceleration written as m/s$^2$ (meters per second, per second)?
choices: Because it measures area, which uses squared units
Because velocity (m/s) changes per second, so it is (m/s) divided by s
Because distance is always squared in physics
Because seconds are multiplied by themselves in every motion
items.explanation: Acceleration is a velocity (in m/s) changing each second, so its unit is (m/s) per s = m/s$^2$. The 'squared' comes from dividing by seconds twice, not from area or from distance being squared.

items.prompt: Two runners move at the same 4 m/s but in opposite directions on a line. Which is true?
choices: They have the same speed but different velocities
They have the same velocity but different speeds
Both their speed and velocity are identical
Neither has a speed because they move opposite ways
items.explanation: Speed ignores direction, so both have speed 4 m/s. Velocity includes direction, so one is +4 m/s and the other -4 m/s: different velocities. Speed certainly still exists for both, so the last option is wrong.

items.prompt: A car braking slows from 20 m/s to 8 m/s in 3 s. What is its acceleration?
items.explanation: Acceleration = (final - initial velocity) / time = (8 - 20) / 3 = -12 / 3 = -4 m/s$^2$. The negative sign shows velocity is decreasing (braking). Forgetting the sign gives +4, and forgetting to divide by time gives -12.

items.prompt: Can an object have a high speed but zero acceleration at some moment?
choices: No, moving fast always means accelerating
Yes, if its velocity is not changing (steady speed, same direction)
No, acceleration and speed are the same thing
Only if it is moving backwards
items.explanation: Acceleration is about *change* in velocity, not how big the velocity is. A car cruising at a steady 30 m/s in a straight line has high speed but zero acceleration. Speed and acceleration are different quantities, so the other options are wrong.

lessons.title: Reading Motion Graphs
lessons.content:
Numbers describe motion, but a graph lets you *see* it at a glance. Two graphs do most of the work in straight-line motion, and the trick to both is the same: pay attention to the **slope** (how steep the line is) and, for one of them, the **area** underneath.

First, a **position-time graph** puts time across the bottom (the horizontal axis) and position up the side (the vertical axis). It shows where the object is at each moment. The slope, meaning rise (change in position) divided by run (change in time), equals the **velocity**. So a steep line means fast, a gentle line means slow, a flat horizontal line means standing still, and a straight slanted line means constant velocity. A line sloping downward means moving in the negative direction.

Second, a **velocity-time graph** puts time across the bottom and velocity up the side. Here the slope equals the **acceleration**: a rising line means speeding up, a flat line means steady velocity, a falling line means slowing down. This graph has a bonus feature: the **area** between the line and the time axis equals the distance travelled, because distance is roughly velocity multiplied by time, and area is height times width.

A quick way to remember: on a position-time graph, slope is velocity; on a velocity-time graph, slope is acceleration and area is distance.

In short: graphs turn motion into pictures, and reading the slope (and area) tells you the velocity, the acceleration, and how far something went.

items.prompt: On a position-time graph, what does the slope of the line represent?
choices: The acceleration
The velocity
The total distance
The starting position
items.explanation: Slope is rise (change in position) over run (change in time), which is exactly velocity. Acceleration is the slope on a velocity-time graph instead, and the starting position is just where the line begins, not its slope.

items.prompt: On a position-time graph, what does a flat, horizontal line tell you?
choices: The object is moving at top speed
The object is standing still
The object is accelerating
The object is moving backward
items.explanation: A horizontal line has zero slope, and slope is velocity, so the velocity is zero: the object is at rest. Top speed would be a steep line, and moving backward would be a downward slope, neither of which is flat.

items.prompt: A position-time graph is a straight line rising from (0 s, 0 m) to (6 s, 30 m). What is the velocity?
choices: 5 m/s
36 m/s
items.explanation: Velocity is the slope = rise / run = 30 m / 6 s = 5 m/s. Multiplying (30 x 6) gives 180, dividing the wrong way gives 0.2, and adding gives 36; only rise divided by run is correct.

items.prompt: On a velocity-time graph, what does the slope of the line represent?
choices: The position
The distance travelled
The acceleration
The speed at the start
items.explanation: On a velocity-time graph the slope is change in velocity over time, which is acceleration. The distance is the area under the line (not the slope), and position is not shown directly on this graph at all.

items.prompt: On a velocity-time graph, what does the area between the line and the time axis represent?
choices: The acceleration
The distance travelled
The maximum velocity
The time taken
items.explanation: Distance is roughly velocity times time, and area is height (velocity) times width (time), so the area equals the distance travelled. The slope, not the area, gives acceleration, and the area is not a single velocity or time value.

items.prompt: A velocity-time graph shows a line rising steadily from 0. What is the object doing?
choices: Standing still
Moving at constant velocity
Speeding up (accelerating)
Slowing down
items.explanation: A rising line means velocity is increasing over time, which is positive acceleration: the object speeds up. A flat line would be constant velocity, a falling line would be slowing down, and standing still would be a line sitting at zero.

items.prompt: On a velocity-time graph, a horizontal line sitting at 8 m/s for 5 s means the object travels how far, and with what acceleration?
choices: 40 m, zero acceleration
1.6 m, high acceleration
13 m, steady acceleration
40 m, large acceleration
items.explanation: A flat line has zero slope, so acceleration is zero. The area is a rectangle: 8 m/s x 5 s = 40 m, the distance travelled. Dividing instead of multiplying gives 1.6 m, and a flat line cannot mean any acceleration, so only the first option fits.

items.prompt: A position-time graph line slopes downward as time increases. What does this mean?
choices: The object is speeding up
The object is moving in the negative direction
The object has stopped
Time is running backwards
items.explanation: A downward slope means position is decreasing over time, so velocity is negative: the object moves in the negative direction. A stop would be a flat line, and time always moves forward, so those options are wrong; steepness, not direction, would indicate speed.

items.prompt: Two position-time lines are both straight, but line A is steeper than line B. What can you conclude?
choices: Object A has a greater velocity than object B
Object A has travelled a longer total time
Object B is accelerating and A is not
Both objects are at rest
items.explanation: On a position-time graph, a steeper slope means a larger velocity, so A is faster than B. Both lines being straight means constant velocity (no acceleration), and steepness has nothing to do with how much time passed or with being at rest.

lessons.title: Constant-Acceleration Equations & Free Fall
lessons.content:
Many real motions have a steady, unchanging acceleration: a car pulling away smoothly, or anything falling near Earth. For these special cases, a small set of formulas lets you predict everything without drawing a graph.

Using $u$ for the starting velocity, $v$ for the final velocity, $a$ for the (constant) acceleration, $t$ for time, and $s$ for displacement, the two most useful equations are:

$$v = u + a\\,t$$ 
The final velocity equals the starting velocity plus how much it grew (acceleration times time).

$$s = u\\,t + \\tfrac{1}{2}\\,a\\,t^2$$ 
The displacement equals the part from the starting speed ($u\\,t$) plus the extra from speeding up.

The star example is **free fall**: motion under gravity alone, ignoring air resistance. Near Earth's surface every object falls with the same downward acceleration, called $g$, approximately **9.8 m/s$^2$**. That means a falling object's speed increases by about 9.8 m/s every second, no matter how heavy it is. A feather and a hammer dropped in a vacuum land together, a famous result.

For a dropped object the starting velocity $u$ is 0, so its speed after time $t$ is just $v = g\\,t$, and the distance fallen is $s = \\tfrac{1}{2} g\\,t^2$. After 1 s it has fallen about 4.9 m; after 2 s, about 19.6 m. The gaps between equal-time positions keep growing because it keeps speeding up.

In short: with constant acceleration, simple equations predict velocity and displacement, and free fall is just constant acceleration with $a = g \\approx 9.8$ m/s$^2$.

items.prompt: In the equation $v = u + a\\,t$, what does $u$ stand for?
choices: The final velocity
The starting (initial) velocity
The acceleration
The displacement
items.explanation: Here $u$ is the initial (starting) velocity, $v$ is the final velocity, $a$ is acceleration, and the displacement is $s$. Mixing up $u$ and $v$ is a common error: the equation says final = initial + (growth from accelerating).

items.prompt: Approximately what is the acceleration due to gravity, g, near Earth's surface?
items.explanation: Near Earth, g is about 9.8 m/s$^2$, an acceleration (note the squared seconds). The choice '9.8 m/s' has the wrong unit (that would be a velocity), and 98 and 1.0 are simply incorrect values.

items.prompt: A ball is dropped from rest. Ignoring air resistance, how fast is it falling after 2 s? (Use g = 9.8 m/s$^2$.)
items.explanation: Dropped from rest means u = 0, so v = g x t = 9.8 x 2 = 19.6 m/s. Using only 9.8 gives the speed after 1 s, not 2 s, and 4.9 confuses speed with the half-factor used for distance.

items.prompt: Ignoring air resistance, how does a heavy rock's falling acceleration compare with a light pebble's?
choices: The rock accelerates faster because it is heavier
They accelerate at the same rate, about 9.8 m/s$^2$
The pebble accelerates faster because it is lighter
Neither accelerates; they fall at constant speed
items.explanation: In free fall, all objects share the same acceleration g regardless of mass, so both fall at about 9.8 m/s$^2$. The belief that heavier means faster is a classic misconception; air resistance (ignored here) is what makes feathers seem slower in everyday life. They do accelerate, not move at constant speed.

items.prompt: A car starts from rest (u = 0) and accelerates at 3 m/s$^2$ for 4 s. What is its final velocity, using $v = u + a\\,t$?
choices: 7 m/s
12 m/s
48 m/s
items.explanation: v = u + a t = 0 + 3 x 4 = 12 m/s. Adding 3 + 4 gives the wrong 7, and dividing (3 / 4) gives 0.75; the equation requires multiplying acceleration by time, then adding the starting velocity (here 0).

items.prompt: A ball dropped from rest falls for 2 s. How far does it fall, using $s = \\tfrac{1}{2} g\\,t^2$ with g = 9.8 m/s$^2$?
choices: about 19.6 m
about 4.9 m
items.explanation: s = 1/2 x 9.8 x (2)^2 = 1/2 x 9.8 x 4 = 19.6 m. Forgetting to square the time gives 9.8 m, and 4.9 m is the distance after only 1 s. Note 'about' since g is rounded. (The plain '19.6 m' option ignores that g is approximate.)

items.prompt: In a free-fall strobe photo, why do the gaps between the ball's positions grow each second?
choices: The ball is getting heavier as it falls
Gravity switches off and on
The ball keeps speeding up, so it covers more distance each second
The camera moves closer to the ball
items.explanation: Constant downward acceleration means the speed keeps increasing, so in each equal time slice the ball travels farther, making the gaps grow. The ball's mass does not change, gravity is steady (not switching off), and the camera is fixed.

items.prompt: What condition must be true for the equation $s = u\\,t + \\tfrac{1}{2} a\\,t^2$ to apply?
choices: The acceleration must be constant (unchanging)
The object must be at rest the whole time
There must be no acceleration at all
The motion must form a circle
items.explanation: These equations are the constant-acceleration equations; they only work when a does not change during the motion. The object need not be at rest (it can start moving), and there must be some defined acceleration. Circular motion is a different topic with changing direction.

items.prompt: A stone is thrown straight up. At the very top of its path, for an instant, what is true?
choices: Its velocity is zero, but gravity still pulls it down at 9.8 m/s$^2$
Both its velocity and its acceleration are zero
Its acceleration points upward
It floats with no forces on it
items.explanation: At the peak the stone momentarily stops rising, so its velocity is zero, but gravity never switches off: the downward acceleration g still acts, which is what pulls it back down. The acceleration is not zero and points down, not up, throughout the flight.

lessons.title: Scalars vs Vectors: Arrows, Magnitude and Direction
lessons.content:
Suppose a friend says "I walked 3 kilometers." Useful, but you still don't know which way they went. Now they say "I walked 3 kilometers north." That extra word, the direction, changes everything. Physics has two kinds of quantities, and this is the difference between them.

A **scalar** is a quantity that is fully described by a single number with a unit, and nothing more. Examples: temperature (20 degrees), mass (5 kilograms), time (10 seconds), or plain distance (3 km). A scalar has size but no direction.

A **vector** is a quantity that needs both a size *and* a direction to be complete. Examples: velocity ("5 m/s east"), force ("a push of 10 newtons downward"), and displacement ("3 km north"). The size part of a vector is called its **magnitude** (just "how big"), and the other part is its **direction** ("which way").

We draw a vector as an **arrow**. The length of the arrow shows the magnitude (a longer arrow means a bigger value), and the way the arrow points shows the direction. The start of the arrow is the **tail**; the pointed end is the **head** or **tip**. So a velocity of 5 m/s east is a short arrow pointing right; 10 m/s east is a longer arrow pointing the same way.

In short: scalars are just a number (size only); vectors are a number *plus* a direction, and we picture them as arrows whose length is the magnitude and whose pointing is the direction.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes a scalar?
choices: A quantity fully described by a number with a unit, and no direction
A quantity that always points north
A quantity that has a direction but no size
An arrow whose length tells you the direction
items.explanation: A scalar is size-only: a number and unit, such as 20 degrees or 5 kg, with no direction attached. It does not point any particular way, so 'always north' is wrong. A quantity with direction but no size is not a real physical quantity, and the arrow-length idea describes vectors, not scalars.

items.prompt: Which of these is a vector?
choices: Temperature of 20 degrees
A mass of 5 kilograms
A velocity of 5 m/s east
A time of 10 seconds
items.explanation: Velocity includes a direction ('east'), so it needs both size and direction, making it a vector. Temperature, mass, and time are all just numbers with units and no direction, so they are scalars. The word 'east' is the giveaway that direction matters.

items.prompt: On a vector arrow, what does the LENGTH of the arrow represent?
choices: The direction of the vector
The magnitude (size) of the vector
The starting point only
The time the motion took
items.explanation: By convention the arrow's length shows the magnitude: a longer arrow means a larger value. Direction is shown by which way the arrow points, not its length. Length has nothing to do with the starting point or with time.

items.prompt: What is the difference between distance and displacement?
choices: They are exactly the same thing
Distance is a vector; displacement is a scalar
Distance is a scalar (size only); displacement is a vector (size and direction)
Displacement is always larger than distance
items.explanation: Distance is total path length with no direction, so it is a scalar; displacement is the net change in position and includes direction, so it is a vector. The labels are not swapped, and displacement can never exceed distance (a straight-line net change is at most the total path walked).

items.prompt: Arrow A is twice as long as arrow B, and both point the same way. What does this tell you?
choices: A has twice the magnitude of B, in the same direction
A points in the opposite direction to B
A and B have equal magnitudes
B is moving faster than A
items.explanation: Length represents magnitude, so a twice-as-long arrow means twice the magnitude; pointing the same way means the same direction. The arrows are not opposite (they point the same way) and not equal in size (one is twice the other).

items.prompt: The pointed end of a vector arrow is called the:
choices: Tail
Origin
Head (or tip)
Scalar
items.explanation: The pointed end is the head or tip; the other (starting) end is the tail. 'Origin' is the zero reference point of a coordinate system, not part of the arrow itself, and 'scalar' is a kind of quantity, not a part of an arrow.

items.prompt: Why is 'speed' a scalar but 'velocity' a vector?
choices: Speed includes direction but velocity does not
Velocity includes direction (e.g. 'east') while speed is just the number
They are unrelated quantities with different units
Speed can be negative but velocity cannot
items.explanation: Velocity is speed plus a direction (for example '5 m/s east'), so it is a vector; speed is only the size, so it is a scalar. The directions are not reversed between the two, they share the same unit (m/s), and it is velocity that can be negative (to show direction), not speed.

items.prompt: Two cars both move at 60 km/h, one heading north and one heading south. How do their speeds and velocities compare?
choices: Same speed and same velocity
Different speeds but same velocity
Same speed but different velocities
Different speeds and different velocities
items.explanation: Speed is size-only, and both are 60 km/h, so the speeds are equal. Velocity includes direction, and north is not south, so the velocities differ. This is exactly why we separate scalars (size) from vectors (size and direction).

lessons.title: Components and Adding Vectors
lessons.content:
Imagine pushing a lawnmower. You push along the handle, but the handle slants down toward the ground. Part of your push moves the mower forward, and part of it presses uselessly into the dirt. Splitting one slanted push into a 'forward part' and a 'downward part' is exactly what **components** do for any vector.

We set up two directions at right angles: a horizontal **x-axis** and a vertical **y-axis**. The **x-component** of a vector is its shadow along the x-axis (how much it points sideways); the **y-component** is its shadow along the y-axis (how much it points up or down). Together, those two pieces rebuild the original arrow.

If a vector has magnitude (length) $r$ and points at an angle $\theta$ (the Greek letter theta, the angle measured from the x-axis), then its components are $r_x = r\cos\theta$ and $r_y = r\sin\theta$. Here $\cos$ and $\sin$ are just functions your calculator gives that turn an angle into a fraction; you don't need to memorize them yet, only know they split the arrow. To go back the other way, the magnitude is $r = \sqrt{r_x^2 + r_y^2}$ (the Pythagorean theorem for a right triangle).

To **add** two vectors, place the tail of the second at the head of the first; the **resultant** (the sum) is the single arrow from the very first tail to the very last head. This is the **tip-to-tail** method. A reliable shortcut: add the x-components together, add the y-components together, and you have the resultant's components.

In short: any vector splits into x and y components, and adding vectors means adding those components (or drawing them tip-to-tail).

items.prompt: What does the 'x-component' of a vector tell you?
choices: How much the vector points along the horizontal x-axis
The total length of the vector
The angle the vector makes
How much the vector points vertically
items.explanation: The x-component is the part of the vector aligned with the horizontal axis, like its sideways shadow. The total length is the magnitude (not the x-component alone), the angle is a separate property, and the vertical part is the y-component.

items.prompt: In the tip-to-tail method, how do you find the resultant of vectors A and B?
choices: Draw an arrow from the tail of A to the head of B (after placing B's tail at A's head)
Subtract B's length from A's length
Draw an arrow from the head of A back to its own tail
Multiply the two arrow lengths together
items.explanation: You put B's tail on A's head, then the resultant runs from the first tail (A's) to the last head (B's). Subtracting lengths ignores direction and is wrong, drawing head-back-to-tail gives the reverse of A, and multiplying lengths is not how vectors add.

items.prompt: A vector has an x-component of 3 and a y-component of 4. What is its magnitude?
items.explanation: Use $r=\sqrt{r_x^2+r_y^2}=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}=5$. The answer 7 is the mistake of just adding 3+4 (components don't add like that for magnitude), and 12 multiplies them, which is also wrong.

items.prompt: Vector A has components (2, 1) and vector B has components (3, 4). What are the components of A + B?
items.explanation: Add x-components and y-components separately: x = 2+3 = 5, y = 1+4 = 5, giving (5, 5). Option (6, 4) multiplies instead of adds; (1, 3) subtracts; (5, 4) forgets to add one of the y-components.

items.prompt: For a vector of magnitude r at angle theta from the x-axis, the x-component is:
choices: $r\sin\theta$
$r\cos\theta$
$r + \theta$
items.explanation: The horizontal (x) component is $r\cos\theta$, and the vertical (y) component is $r\sin\theta$. Swapping them (using sine for x) is a very common slip. Adding $r+\theta$ mixes a length with an angle, which makes no physical sense.

items.prompt: Why is breaking a vector into components useful?
choices: It changes the vector into a scalar
It lets you handle the horizontal and vertical parts separately, then combine them
It makes the vector point north
It removes the need for any direction
items.explanation: Components let us treat the sideways and up/down motion independently with simple arithmetic, then recombine. It does not turn a vector into a scalar, does not force any particular direction, and the direction information is preserved in the two components.

items.prompt: Two displacement vectors point in exactly opposite directions, one of magnitude 7 and one of magnitude 4. What is the magnitude of their resultant?
items.explanation: Opposite directions partly cancel: along one line, 7 - 4 = 3. Adding to 11 would be correct only if they pointed the SAME way. 28 multiplies them, and 0 would require them to be equal in size.

items.prompt: A vector points straight up the y-axis. What is its x-component?
choices: Equal to its full magnitude
Zero
Half its magnitude
Negative
items.explanation: Pointing straight up means it has no sideways part, so the x-component is zero and the y-component equals the full magnitude. It is not the full magnitude (that is the y-component here), not half, and not negative.

items.prompt: When you add the same two vectors in the opposite order (B + A instead of A + B), the resultant is:
choices: The same as A + B
Always pointing the opposite way
Always zero
Twice as long
items.explanation: Vector addition does not depend on order, so A + B and B + A give the identical resultant (you can draw the parallelogram both ways). It is not reversed, not zero, and not doubled.

lessons.title: Projectile Motion
lessons.content:
Throw a ball forward and watch it curve down to the ground. That graceful arc is a **projectile**, which simply means any object moving through the air with only gravity acting on it (we ignore air resistance for now). The surprising secret is that we can split the motion into two simple, independent stories.

**Independence of motion** is the key idea: the horizontal (sideways) motion and the vertical (up/down) motion do not affect each other. Gravity, the pull of the Earth that makes things fall, acts only downward. So gravity changes the vertical motion but leaves the horizontal motion completely alone.

Horizontally, there is no force (we ignore air), so the **horizontal velocity** (sideways speed) stays *constant* the whole flight. Vertically, gravity acts, so the **vertical velocity** changes steadily: the object slows as it rises, stops rising for an instant at the top, then speeds up as it falls. The acceleration from gravity near Earth is about $g = 9.8\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$ downward (acceleration means how fast the velocity changes).

A famous demonstration: a bullet fired horizontally and a bullet simply dropped from the same height hit the ground at the *same time*, because both have the same downward gravity story and the sideways motion does not delay the fall.

In short: a projectile has constant horizontal velocity and a vertical velocity governed by gravity, and because the two are independent, you can solve each one separately and then combine them.

items.prompt: What is a 'projectile' in physics?
choices: Any object moving through the air with only gravity acting on it
An object that moves only in a straight horizontal line
An object resting on the ground
Any object with an engine pushing it
items.explanation: A projectile is launched and then moves under gravity alone (ignoring air resistance). A purely horizontal mover or a resting object is not following a projectile arc, and an engine-driven object has an extra force, so it is not a simple projectile.

items.prompt: Ignoring air resistance, what happens to a projectile's horizontal velocity during flight?
choices: It steadily increases
It steadily decreases
It stays constant
It drops to zero at the top of the arc
items.explanation: With no horizontal force (air ignored), the sideways speed never changes, so it stays constant. Gravity acts only vertically, so it cannot speed up or slow down the horizontal motion. The vertical velocity, not the horizontal one, is zero at the top.

items.prompt: At the very highest point of a projectile's arc, its VERTICAL velocity is:
choices: At its maximum
Zero for an instant
Equal to the horizontal velocity
Pointing upward strongly
items.explanation: Going up, the object slows until its vertical velocity reaches zero at the peak, then it starts falling. It is not at a maximum there (that is at launch or landing), and there is no reason it must equal the horizontal velocity.

items.prompt: A bullet is fired horizontally while an identical bullet is dropped from the same height at the same moment. Which hits the ground first?
choices: The fired bullet, because it moves faster
The dropped bullet, because it goes straight down
They hit at the same time
Neither, the fired bullet never lands
items.explanation: Because horizontal and vertical motion are independent, both bullets have the identical downward gravity story and fall for the same time, hitting together. Moving sideways does not delay the fall, and a fired projectile does eventually land.

items.prompt: Which direction does gravity act on a projectile?
choices: Always downward
In the direction the object is moving
Sideways, opposing the horizontal motion
Upward while the object rises
items.explanation: Gravity always pulls straight down toward the Earth, regardless of which way the projectile happens to be moving. It does not follow the motion, does not oppose horizontal motion (that would be air resistance), and never points upward.

items.prompt: What does it mean that horizontal and vertical motions are 'independent'?
choices: The two motions happen at different times
What happens vertically does not change the horizontal motion, and vice versa
Only one of the two motions can occur at once
The object must move in a perfect circle
items.explanation: Independence means you can analyze each direction separately because neither affects the other. The motions happen at the same time, not at different times, and both occur together, producing the curved path, not a circle.

items.prompt: The value g near Earth's surface is about 9.8 m/s^2. What does it represent?
choices: The constant speed of every falling object
How fast the downward velocity changes each second due to gravity
The horizontal speed of a projectile
The total distance an object falls
items.explanation: g is an acceleration: each second, a falling object's downward velocity grows by about 9.8 m/s. It is not a fixed speed (speed keeps increasing while falling), not the horizontal speed, and not a distance.

items.prompt: Two balls are launched horizontally off the same cliff, one twice as fast as the other. Compared to the slower ball, the faster ball:
choices: Lands much sooner
Lands at the same time but travels farther horizontally
Lands later because it must travel farther
Never comes down
items.explanation: Both fall from the same height with the same vertical gravity story, so they land at the same time; the faster ball just covers more horizontal distance during that equal fall time. Horizontal speed does not change the fall time.

lessons.title: Introduction to Uniform Circular Motion
lessons.content:
Swing a ball on a string in a circle, or feel yourself pressed sideways as a car rounds a bend. The object keeps the same speed but keeps changing where it heads. This is **uniform circular motion**: moving in a circle at a *constant speed*. ("Uniform" here means the speed stays the same.)

Here is the twist that trips up almost everyone. Even at constant speed, the object is **accelerating**. Recall that velocity is a vector, meaning it has direction; **acceleration** is any change in velocity, including a change in *direction* alone. As the object goes around, its direction is constantly turning, so its velocity is constantly changing, so it is accelerating, even though its speed never changes.

Which way does the velocity point? Always along the **tangent**, the straight line that just grazes the circle at that point, pointing the way the object is instantly moving. If the string snapped, the ball would fly off along that tangent line, not outward along the radius.

Which way does the acceleration point? Always toward the **center** of the circle. We call this inward acceleration **centripetal acceleration**, from Latin words meaning "center-seeking." Something must constantly pull the object inward (the string's tension, friction on tires, gravity for a satellite) to keep bending its path into a circle.

In short: in uniform circular motion the speed is constant, the velocity points along the tangent, and the centripetal acceleration always points inward toward the center.

items.prompt: What does 'uniform' mean in 'uniform circular motion'?
choices: The radius keeps shrinking
The speed stays constant
The object moves in a straight line
There is no acceleration
items.explanation: 'Uniform' refers to constant speed around the circle. The radius is fixed (not shrinking), the path is a circle not a straight line, and there IS acceleration because the direction keeps changing.

items.prompt: How can an object moving at constant speed still be accelerating?
choices: It cannot; constant speed means zero acceleration
Because its direction (and therefore its velocity) is changing
Because its mass is changing
Because gravity disappears in a circle
items.explanation: Acceleration is any change in velocity, and velocity includes direction; turning continuously changes the direction even at constant speed, so there is acceleration. Mass is not changing and gravity does not disappear.

items.prompt: In which direction does the velocity of an object in circular motion point?
choices: Toward the center of the circle
Away from the center (outward)
Along the tangent, the way it is instantly moving
Straight up
items.explanation: Velocity is always tangent to the circle, pointing the way the object is moving at that instant. Toward the center is the acceleration's direction, outward is a common misconception, and 'straight up' has nothing to do with the circle's plane.

items.prompt: In which direction does centripetal acceleration point?
choices: Toward the center of the circle
Outward, away from the center
Along the tangent
Backward, opposite the motion
items.explanation: 'Centripetal' means center-seeking, so the acceleration always points inward toward the center. Outward is the popular but wrong 'centrifugal' feeling, the tangent is the velocity's direction, and 'backward' would only slow the object, not bend its path.

items.prompt: If the string on a ball swung in a horizontal circle suddenly snaps, which path does the ball follow right after?
choices: It continues in a circle for a while
It flies straight outward along the radius
It flies off along the tangent (a straight line)
It drops straight down immediately with no sideways motion
items.explanation: With no inward force, the ball keeps the velocity it had, which is tangent to the circle, so it travels in a straight tangent line. It does not keep curving (nothing pulls it in), and it does not shoot straight out along the radius.

items.prompt: What keeps a car turning around a flat curve, providing the centripetal (inward) acceleration?
choices: Friction between the tires and the road, directed toward the center
An outward force pushing the car away from the center
The car's constant speed
Air resistance from the front
items.explanation: Friction acts inward on the tires, supplying the center-pointing force needed to curve the path. There is no real outward force (the 'outward push' is just the body's inertia), speed alone is not a force, and air resistance opposes forward motion, not the turn.

items.prompt: At any moment in uniform circular motion, the angle between the velocity and the centripetal acceleration is:
choices: 0 degrees (same direction)
180 degrees (opposite directions)
90 degrees (at right angles)
45 degrees
items.explanation: Velocity points along the tangent and acceleration points toward the center, and a radius meets its tangent at a right angle, so they are 90 degrees apart. They are not parallel (0 or 180 degrees) nor at 45 degrees. This right angle is why speed stays constant while direction turns.

items.prompt: Why do you feel 'pushed outward' when a car turns sharply, even though the real acceleration points inward?
choices: A real outward force acts on your body
Your body's inertia tends to keep going straight while the car turns inward beneath you
Gravity reverses direction during the turn
The centripetal acceleration actually points outward
items.explanation: Your body tends to keep moving straight (inertia), so as the car curves inward the door comes to meet you, feeling like an outward push. There is no genuine outward force, gravity does not reverse, and the centripetal acceleration genuinely points inward.

lessons.title: What a Force Is, and Newton's First Law
lessons.content:
Why do things start moving, stop, speed up, or change direction? The answer is almost always a **force**. Forces are everywhere: when you open a door, kick a ball, or sit in a chair, forces are at work.

**Build:** A force is simply a **push or a pull** on an object. That's it. Kicking a ball is a push; tugging a rope is a pull. Forces have a **size** (how strong) and a **direction** (which way). We measure the size in a unit called the **newton** (symbol **N**), named after the scientist Isaac Newton. As a feel for it, holding a small apple takes about 1 N.

Now the big idea, **Newton's First Law** (also called the law of **inertia**): an object will keep doing what it is already doing unless a force changes it. A still object stays still; a moving object keeps moving in a straight line at a steady speed, *until a force acts on it*. **Inertia** is just this tendency of objects to resist changes in their motion. Heavier objects have more inertia.

A common trap: people think motion itself needs a constant force to keep going. It does not. A puck slides forever on perfect frictionless ice with no push at all. On real ice it slows only because **friction** (a force) acts on it. No force needed to *keep* moving, only to *change* the motion.

**Recap:** A force is a push or pull measured in newtons. With no net force, an object keeps its current motion. That is Newton's First Law, the law of inertia.

items.prompt: In physics, what exactly is a force?
choices: A push or a pull on an object
Only the speed of an object
The weight of an object alone
A type of energy stored in motion
items.explanation: A force is simply a push or a pull. Speed and weight are related ideas but are not the definition of force. Energy is a separate concept.

items.prompt: What is the unit used to measure the size of a force?
choices: The newton (N)
The kilogram (kg)
The meter (m)
The second (s)
items.explanation: Force is measured in newtons (N). The kilogram measures mass, the meter measures distance, and the second measures time.

items.prompt: A hockey puck slides across perfectly frictionless ice at a steady speed in a straight line. What force is needed to KEEP it moving like that?
choices: No force at all
A constant forward push
A force equal to its weight
A pull in the direction it moves
items.explanation: This targets the classic misconception that motion needs a force to continue. By Newton's First Law, with no net force an object keeps moving in a straight line at steady speed. A force is only needed to CHANGE the motion.

items.prompt: What does 'inertia' mean?
choices: An object's tendency to resist changes in its motion
The force of gravity pulling things down
The speed an object is moving at
The amount of friction on a surface
items.explanation: Inertia is the resistance of an object to changes in its motion. It is not a force itself and not the same as speed or gravity.

items.prompt: Which object has the MOST inertia?
choices: A loaded freight truck
A bicycle
A tennis ball
A sheet of paper
items.explanation: More mass means more inertia, so the heavy freight truck resists changes in motion the most. That is why a truck is so hard to start moving and so hard to stop.

items.prompt: A book rests still on a table and stays still. What does Newton's First Law say about this?
choices: It stays still because no net force is acting to change its motion
It stays still because objects naturally prefer to be at rest
It stays still because gravity has been turned off
It stays still only because the table is heavier than the book
items.explanation: An object at rest stays at rest when there is no net (overall) force changing its motion. Gravity still pulls down, but the table pushes up equally, so the forces cancel.

items.prompt: Roughly how big is a force of 1 newton in everyday terms?
choices: About the weight of a small apple held in your hand
About the weight of a car
About the push needed to move a mountain
Too tiny for any object to feel
items.explanation: One newton is roughly the weight of a small apple. This everyday feel helps you judge whether a force value is small or large.

items.prompt: A car cruising on a highway suddenly slows down. According to Newton's First Law, what must be true?
choices: Some force (like braking or friction) acted on it
It ran out of inertia
No explanation is needed, since moving things naturally slow
Its mass decreased
items.explanation: A change in motion always means a net force acted, here the brakes and friction. Objects do not 'run out of inertia,' and motion does not naturally fade without a force.

items.prompt: Two forces act on a box: 5 N pushing right and 5 N pushing left. What is the net (overall) force, and what happens?
choices: 0 N net; the box's motion does not change
10 N net to the right; the box speeds up
5 N net; the box drifts left
The box must fall down
items.explanation: Equal and opposite forces cancel, giving zero net force. With no net force, motion does not change, exactly what the First Law predicts.

lessons.title: Newton's Second Law (F = m a), Mass vs Weight
lessons.content:
The First Law tells us a force *changes* motion. Newton's **Second Law** tells us *how much*. This is the workhorse equation of physics, and it explains why a light cart is easy to push but a loaded one is not.

**Build:** When a net force acts on an object, the object **accelerates**, meaning its motion changes (it speeds up, slows down, or turns). The rule is:

$$F = m \\times a$$

In words: net **force** (F, in newtons) equals **mass** (m, in kilograms) times **acceleration** (a, in meters per second squared, written $\\text{m/s}^2$). Two patterns fall out. For the same object, a **bigger push gives bigger acceleration**. For the same push, a **heavier object accelerates less**, because mass resists changes in motion.

Now a crucial distinction. **Mass** is the amount of 'stuff' in an object, measured in kilograms; it never changes no matter where you are. **Weight** is the *force* of gravity pulling on that mass, measured in newtons, and it changes with location. On Earth, weight = mass times $g$, where $g \\approx 9.8\\,\\text{m/s}^2$. So a 10 kg bag has a weight of about 98 N on Earth.

A common mistake: saying something 'weighs 10 kg.' Kilograms measure mass, not weight. On the Moon that same 10 kg of stuff is unchanged, but it *weighs* about one sixth as much because the Moon's gravity is weaker.

**Recap:** $F = m a$ links force, mass, and acceleration. Mass (kg) is constant; weight (N) is the pull of gravity and depends on where you are.

items.prompt: What does Newton's Second Law, F = m a, relate?
choices: Net force, mass, and acceleration
Weight, height, and time
Speed, distance, and friction
Push, pull, and color
items.explanation: F = m a connects the net force on an object to its mass and its acceleration. The other options mix in unrelated quantities.

items.prompt: You push two boxes with the same force, but box B has twice the mass of box A. How do their accelerations compare?
choices: Box A accelerates twice as much as box B
They accelerate the same
Box B accelerates twice as much as box A
Box B does not move at all
items.explanation: From a = F / m, more mass means less acceleration for the same force. Doubling the mass halves the acceleration, so the lighter box A accelerates twice as much.

items.prompt: A net force of 10 N acts on a 2 kg cart. What is its acceleration?
items.explanation: Rearrange F = m a to a = F / m = 10 N / 2 kg = 5 m/s^2. Multiplying instead of dividing (giving 20) is the common slip.

items.prompt: What is the difference between mass and weight?
choices: Mass is the amount of stuff (kg); weight is the force of gravity on it (N)
They are exactly the same thing
Mass is measured in newtons; weight in kilograms
Weight never changes but mass does
items.explanation: Mass measures how much matter is in an object (kilograms) and stays constant. Weight is the gravitational force on that mass (newtons) and changes with gravity.

items.prompt: An astronaut takes a 10 kg toolbox to the Moon. What happens to its mass and weight?
choices: Mass stays 10 kg; weight becomes smaller
Both mass and weight become smaller
Both stay exactly the same
Mass becomes smaller; weight stays the same
items.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter and does not change with location, so it stays 10 kg. Weight is gravity's pull, and the Moon's weaker gravity makes it weigh about one sixth as much.

items.prompt: On Earth (g approximately 9.8 m/s^2), what is the approximate weight of a 5 kg object?
choices: About 49 N
About 5 N
About 9.8 N
About 0.5 N
items.explanation: Weight = mass x g = 5 kg x 9.8 m/s^2, which is about 49 N. Reporting 5 confuses mass (kg) with weight (N).

items.prompt: Why is it not quite right to say a bag of flour 'weighs 2 kilograms'?
choices: Kilograms measure mass, not weight; weight is a force in newtons
Because flour has no weight at all
Because only liquids can be weighed
Because kilograms only apply on the Moon
items.explanation: Strictly, kilograms measure mass. The bag's weight (the force of gravity on it) would be in newtons. Everyday speech blurs this, but physics keeps them separate.

items.prompt: You double the net force on a cart while keeping its mass the same. What happens to its acceleration?
choices: It doubles
It stays the same
It is cut in half
It becomes zero
items.explanation: Since a = F / m, doubling F with m unchanged doubles a. Force and acceleration are directly proportional for a fixed mass.

items.prompt: A 4 kg object accelerates at 3 m/s^2. What net force is acting on it?
choices: 7 N
items.explanation: Use F = m a = 4 kg x 3 m/s^2 = 12 N. Adding the numbers (7) or dividing them are the typical errors.

lessons.title: Newton's Third Law: Action and Reaction
lessons.content:
Have you ever pushed off a wall while on roller skates and rolled backward? You pushed the wall, yet *you* moved. Newton's **Third Law** explains this, and it is the most misunderstood of the three.

**Build:** The law states: for every action, there is an **equal and opposite reaction**. More precisely, whenever object A pushes or pulls object B, object B pushes or pulls back on A with a force that is **equal in size** and **opposite in direction**. Forces always come in **pairs**.

The key, often-missed point: the two forces in a pair act on **different objects**. When your foot pushes down on the ground, the ground pushes up on your foot. That upward push on you is what lets you walk and jump. A swimmer pushes water backward; the water pushes the swimmer forward. A rocket pushes gas out the back; the gas pushes the rocket forward.

Now the big misconception: people think the action-reaction pair cancels out, so nothing should ever move. They do **not** cancel, because they act on **two different objects**.
