items.explanation: Sound covers about 1 km every 3 seconds, so 6 seconds means roughly 2 km. 100 km (choice 2) would take far longer; a 6-second gap means it is not right next to you (choice 3); and the timing method genuinely works as a rough estimate (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the equation $v = f\\lambda$, the symbol $\\lambda$ (lambda) stands for:
choices: The loudness of the sound
The wavelength — the length of one full wave
The number of waves per second
The speed of the wave
items.explanation: Lambda is the wavelength, the length of a single complete wave. Loudness (choice 1) is not in this equation; waves per second is the frequency $f$ (choice 3); and the speed is $v$ (choice 4).

items.prompt: Compared with the speed of light, the speed of sound in air is:
choices: Slightly faster
About the same
Far slower
Exactly half as fast
items.explanation: Light travels about 300 million m/s versus sound's ~343 m/s — sound is hundreds of thousands of times slower. It is nowhere near faster, equal, or half (choices 1, 2, 4).

items.prompt: Two friends tap a long metal pipe; one listens with an ear pressed to the metal, the other listens through the air the same distance away. What happens?
choices: The listener on the metal hears the tap first, because sound is faster in the solid
The listener in the air hears it first, because air is faster
They hear it at exactly the same instant
Neither hears anything, because metal blocks sound
items.explanation: Sound speeds through steel (~5000 m/s) far faster than through air (~343 m/s), so the metal listener hears it first. Air is the slower medium (choice 2), the speeds differ so the timing differs (choice 3), and metal carries sound well rather than blocking it (choice 4).

lessons.title: Doppler Effect, Resonance & Beats
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A siren races past: as it approaches the pitch sounds high, and the instant it passes, the pitch drops — 'eeeeee-ooooow.' The driver inside hears no change at all. What gives?

**Build.** This is the **Doppler effect**: when a sound source moves toward you, each new wave is sent from a little closer than the last, so the compressions arrive bunched together — more waves per second, meaning a higher *perceived* frequency, so higher pitch. As the source moves away, the waves are stretched apart, fewer arrive per second, and you hear a lower pitch. Crucial point and a common trap: the source's *true* frequency never changes — only the frequency you *perceive* changes because of the motion. The driver, moving with the siren, hears it normally.

Now, how do instruments make steady musical notes? Through **standing waves** and **resonance**. A guitar string or the air in a flute can only vibrate comfortably at certain special frequencies set by its length; at those frequencies the reflected waves reinforce each other into a stable, fixed pattern called a **standing wave**. **Resonance** is this natural strong response at a favored frequency — it is why a short string or short pipe gives a high note and a long one gives a low note.

Finally, **beats**: play two notes whose frequencies are *almost* equal and you hear a slow throbbing 'wah-wah-wah' as they alternately reinforce and cancel. Musicians use beats to tune — the throb slows and vanishes when the two pitches match exactly.

**Recap.** Doppler shifts *perceived* pitch with motion (source unchanged); standing waves and resonance let instruments make set notes; near-equal frequencies make beats.

items.prompt: As an ambulance siren moves toward you, you hear its pitch as:
choices: Lower than normal
Higher than normal
Exactly normal, with no change
Silent until it passes
items.explanation: An approaching source bunches its waves, so more arrive per second and the perceived pitch rises. A receding source would sound lower (choice 1). Motion does change the perceived pitch (choices 3 and 4 are wrong).

items.prompt: In the Doppler effect, what actually changes when a siren approaches and then recedes?
choices: The true frequency produced by the siren itself
Only the frequency perceived by the listener; the source's true frequency is unchanged
Both the true frequency and the loudness disappear
The speed of sound in the air around the siren
items.explanation: The siren keeps emitting the same frequency; motion changes how often waves reach the listener, shifting the perceived pitch. The source frequency does not actually change (choice 1), nor does sound's speed in the medium (choice 4); choice 3 misstates what happens.

items.prompt: Why does the driver inside the ambulance hear the siren at a steady, normal pitch?
choices: Because the driver is moving along with the source, so the waves are not bunched or stretched for them
Because the siren shuts off its Doppler effect for the driver
Because the inside of the ambulance is a vacuum
Because the driver's ears cancel out the change
items.explanation: There is no relative motion between the driver and the source, so no Doppler shift occurs for them. The effect cannot be 'switched off' (choice 2), the cab is full of air (choice 3), and ears do not auto-cancel pitch shifts (choice 4).

items.prompt: As a sound source moves AWAY from a listener, the waves reaching that listener are:
choices: Bunched closer together, raising the pitch
Stretched farther apart, lowering the pitch
Unchanged in spacing
Stopped completely
items.explanation: A receding source leaves each wave a bit farther behind, stretching them out so fewer arrive per second — a lower perceived pitch. Bunching and higher pitch (choice 1) happen when approaching. The spacing does change (choice 3), and the sound does not stop (choice 4).

items.prompt: A 'standing wave' on a guitar string is:
choices: A wave that travels off the end of the string into the room
A stable, fixed vibration pattern formed when reflected waves reinforce at certain frequencies
A string that is not moving at all
The loudness of the plucked note
items.explanation: At special frequencies set by the string's length, waves reflecting back and forth reinforce into a steady pattern that appears to 'stand still.' It is not a wave leaving the string (choice 1), not a motionless string (choice 3), and not loudness (choice 4).

items.prompt: On a guitar, pressing a string shorter (so a smaller length vibrates) produces:
choices: A lower note, because shorter means slower
A higher note, because shorter lengths resonate at higher frequencies
The same note, because length does not matter
A louder note, but the same pitch
items.explanation: Shorter vibrating lengths resonate at higher frequencies, giving higher notes — that is how fretting works. Shorter does not mean lower (choice 1); length strongly affects pitch (choice 3); and the change is in pitch, not just loudness (choice 4).

items.prompt: 'Resonance' in a musical instrument refers to:
choices: An object's natural strong vibration at certain favored frequencies
The decibel level of the loudest possible note
The Doppler shift heard when the instrument moves
The time it takes a note to fade to silence
items.explanation: Resonance is the large, sustained response a system gives at its preferred (natural) frequencies, which is how strings and air columns build clear notes. It is not a loudness measure (choice 2), not Doppler motion (choice 3), and not the fade time (choice 4).

items.prompt: When two notes of ALMOST equal frequency play together, you hear a slow throbbing called 'beats' because the waves:
choices: Travel at different speeds through the air
Alternately reinforce (get louder) and cancel (get softer) as they drift in and out of step
Change the speed of sound around them
Merge into a single much higher note
items.explanation: Slightly mismatched frequencies repeatedly fall into and out of step, so the combined sound swells and fades — the throb we call beats, useful for tuning. The notes travel at the same speed (choice 1), do not alter sound's speed (choice 3), and do not fuse into one higher note (choice 4).

lessons.title: Temperature vs Heat
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A sparkler glowing at thousands of degrees can shower your hand and barely sting, yet a pot of boiling water at a 'mere' 100 degrees would scald you badly. How can the cooler thing be the dangerous one? Because 'how hot' and 'how much heat' are two different ideas.

**Build.** Everything is made of tiny particles called **molecules** (the smallest pieces of a substance) that are always jiggling. **Temperature** measures the *average* jiggling speed of those molecules — in plain words, how hot or cold something is. Faster average jiggling means a higher temperature. A sparkler's few specks jiggle furiously (high temperature) but there are so few of them that they carry little total energy.

**Heat** is different: heat is *energy that flows* from a hotter object to a cooler one because of their temperature difference. The boiling water holds a huge amount of this energy, so it transfers a lot of heat into your skin. Key idea: heat always moves from hot to cold, never the other way on its own.

We label temperature with **scales**. **Celsius (C)**: water freezes at 0 and boils at 100. **Fahrenheit (F)**: water freezes at 32 and boils at 212. **Kelvin (K)**: the science scale, which starts at **absolute zero** (about −273 C) — the coldest possible point, where molecular jiggling all but stops. There are no negative kelvin temperatures.

**Recap.** Temperature is the *average* molecular motion (how hot); heat is *energy that flows* from hot to cold. Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin are just different rulers for temperature.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between temperature and heat?
choices: Temperature is the average molecular motion (how hot); heat is energy that flows from hot to cold
Temperature and heat are two words for exactly the same thing
Heat is how hot something is; temperature is the energy that flows
Temperature only applies to gases, heat only to solids
items.explanation: Temperature gauges the average jiggling speed of molecules, while heat is energy in transit from a hotter to a cooler object. Choice 2 ignores their real difference; choice 3 swaps the two definitions; choice 4 invents a limit that does not exist.

items.prompt: A tiny spark at 1000 C lands on your hand and barely hurts, but 100 C boiling water badly scalds you. Why?
choices: The spark must actually be cooler than the thermometer says
The boiling water, though lower in temperature, carries far more total heat energy into your skin
Heat flows from your cool hand into the hot spark, protecting you
Temperature has no effect on how much something burns
items.explanation: Temperature (how hot) and heat (total energy transferred) are different: the spark is hotter but holds almost no energy, while the large mass of boiling water delivers a lot of heat. Choice 1 misreads the thermometer; choice 3 reverses heat flow; choice 4 wrongly dismisses temperature entirely.

items.prompt: When you hold a warm mug, in which direction does heat actually flow?
choices: Cold flows from your hand into the mug, cooling the mug
Heat flows from the warmer mug into your cooler hand
Nothing flows; warmth just appears in your hand on its own
Heat flows both ways equally, so nothing changes
items.explanation: Heat always travels from hotter to cooler, so energy leaves the mug and enters your hand. The common 'cold flows in' idea (choice 1) is backwards — there is no separate substance called cold. Choice 3 ignores the energy transfer, and choice 4 contradicts the one-way nature of spontaneous heat flow.

items.prompt: On the Celsius scale, water freezes and boils at:
choices: Freezes at 32, boils at 212
Freezes at 0, boils at 100
Freezes at 273, boils at 373
Freezes at -273, boils at 0
items.explanation: Celsius sets water's freezing point at 0 and boiling point at 100. The 32/212 pair (choice 1) is Fahrenheit, the 273/373 pair (choice 3) is Kelvin, and choice 4 misplaces both points.

items.prompt: 'Absolute zero' (0 kelvin) is best described as:
choices: The temperature at which water freezes
Room temperature on the science scale
The coldest possible temperature, where molecular motion all but stops
The hottest temperature anything can reach
items.explanation: Absolute zero is the lowest temperature possible — molecules have essentially no jiggling energy left to remove. It is far below water's freezing point (choice 1), nowhere near room temperature (choice 2), and is a lower limit, not an upper one (choice 4).

items.prompt: If the molecules in a substance start jiggling faster on average, the substance's temperature:
choices: Rises, because temperature measures average molecular motion
Falls, because faster molecules feel colder
Stays the same, because motion and temperature are unrelated
Becomes negative on the Kelvin scale
items.explanation: Temperature tracks the average speed of molecular jiggling, so faster motion means a higher temperature. Choice 2 reverses this; choice 3 denies the link that defines temperature; and Kelvin has no negative values (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which scale is the 'science' scale that begins at absolute zero?
choices: Fahrenheit
Celsius
Kelvin
Decibel
items.explanation: Kelvin starts at absolute zero and never goes negative, which is why scientists favor it. Fahrenheit (choice 1) and Celsius (choice 2) both allow negative readings, and the decibel (choice 4) measures loudness, not temperature.

items.prompt: Two identical cups of coffee are poured. One is left for an hour and cools to room temperature. What happened to its heat?
choices: Coldness from the room flowed into the coffee
Heat energy flowed out of the warm coffee into the cooler room until they matched
The coffee's molecules disappeared, lowering the temperature
Heat was destroyed, which is why the temperature dropped
items.explanation: Heat flows from hot to cold, so energy left the coffee and warmed the room until temperatures evened out. There is no 'coldness' substance flowing in (choice 1); molecules are not lost (choice 3); and energy is not destroyed, only transferred (choice 4).

lessons.title: Heat Transfer: Conduction, Convection, Radiation
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A metal spoon left in hot soup soon burns your fingers, the air above a radiator drifts warm across the room, and the Sun heats your face from 150 million kilometers away through empty space. Three everyday scenes, three different ways heat travels.

**Build.** **Conduction** is heat moving through direct contact, passing molecule to molecule. Fast-jiggling hot molecules bump their slower neighbors and speed them up, like a nudge passed down a packed line of people. Materials that pass heat easily — most metals — are **conductors**; materials that resist it — wood, plastic, air, wool — are **insulators**. This explains a famous puzzle: a metal railing and a wooden bench in the same cold room are at the same temperature, yet the metal feels colder. The metal conducts heat out of your hand quickly, so your skin cools fast; the wood barely conducts, so it feels warmer. The metal is not actually colder — it just steals your heat faster.

**Convection** is heat carried by a moving **fluid** (a liquid or gas). Warm fluid expands, becomes lighter, and rises; cooler fluid sinks to take its place, setting up a circulating loop. This is why hot air rises and why boiling water churns.

**Radiation** is heat carried by invisible waves (called infrared) that need no material at all — which is how the Sun's energy crosses the vacuum of space to reach us. Anything warm radiates; the hotter it is, the more it radiates.

**Recap.** Conduction = touch (solids); convection = moving fluids; radiation = waves through empty space. Conductors pass heat readily; insulators slow it down.

items.prompt: A metal spoon sitting in hot soup gradually heats up along its whole length. This is an example of:
choices: Convection
Conduction
Radiation
Evaporation
items.explanation: Heat passes molecule to molecule through the solid metal by direct contact — that is conduction. Convection (choice 1) needs a moving fluid; radiation (choice 3) is waves through space; evaporation (choice 4) is liquid turning to vapor, not heat transfer along a solid.

items.prompt: A metal railing and a wooden bench have sat in the same cold room all night. Why does the metal feel colder to your hand?
choices: The metal really is at a lower temperature than the wood
Metal conducts heat away from your hand much faster, cooling your skin quickly
Wood gives off coldness more slowly than metal
Your hand warms the wood but not the metal
items.explanation: Both are at the same temperature, but metal is a far better conductor, so it pulls heat out of your skin rapidly and feels colder. They are not at different temperatures (choice 1); 'coldness' is not a substance that flows (choice 3); and your hand warms whatever it touches (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which of these is the best everyday example of convection?
choices: A spoon heating up in a hot drink
Warm air rising off a radiator and circulating around a room
Sunlight warming your face on a winter day
A wooden handle staying cool on a hot pan
items.explanation: Convection is heat carried by a moving fluid: warm air rises, cool air sinks, and a circulating loop spreads the heat. The hot spoon is conduction (choice 1), sunlight is radiation (choice 3), and the cool handle illustrates insulation, not convection (choice 4).

items.prompt: How does the Sun's heat reach Earth across the vacuum of space?
choices: By conduction through space dust
By convection currents in the vacuum
By radiation — waves that need no material medium
It does not; the warmth is generated only in our atmosphere
items.explanation: Radiation travels as waves and needs no matter, so it crosses empty space easily. Conduction (choice 1) and convection (choice 2) both require a material medium, which a vacuum lacks, and the Sun clearly does heat us from afar (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which material is the best INSULATOR (slows heat down the most)?
choices: Copper
Aluminum
Wool
Iron
items.explanation: Wool traps air and conducts heat poorly, making it a good insulator — hence woolly sweaters. Copper, aluminum, and iron are metals and excellent conductors, so they let heat through quickly.

items.prompt: In convection, why does the warm part of a fluid rise?
choices: Warm fluid expands and becomes lighter, so it floats up while cooler fluid sinks
Heat physically pushes the fluid upward with a force
Cold fluid is always lighter than warm fluid
Fluids cannot actually move, so nothing rises
items.explanation: Heating makes a fluid expand and become less dense, so it rises while denser, cooler fluid sinks, creating a circulating loop. Heat does not shove fluid with a direct force (choice 2); cold fluid is denser and sinks (choice 3); and fluids clearly do move (choice 4).

items.prompt: A 'conductor' of heat is a material that:
choices: Blocks heat from passing through it
Lets heat pass through it easily, like most metals
Only works for electricity, never heat
Turns heat into light
items.explanation: A heat conductor passes thermal energy readily; metals are the classic example. A material that blocks heat is an insulator (choice 1); conduction applies to heat as well as electricity (choice 3); and conducting heat does not create light (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why are pot handles often made of wood or plastic rather than metal?
choices: Because wood and plastic generate their own coldness
Because they are insulators, conducting heat slowly so the handle stays cool enough to hold
Because metal cannot be shaped into handles
Because wood radiates heat away faster than metal does
items.explanation: Wood and plastic are poor conductors (good insulators), so heat from the pot reaches your hand slowly and the handle stays grippable. They do not make coldness (choice 1); metal can be shaped (choice 3); and the point is slow conduction, not fast radiation (choice 4).

lessons.title: Specific Heat, Expansion & Phase Changes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** At the beach, sand burns your feet by noon while the sea stays cool — same sunshine on both. And a pot of water can sit at a rolling boil for ten minutes without ever getting hotter than 100 C. Two clues that heat does more than just 'raise temperature.'

**Build.** **Specific heat** is the amount of heat energy needed to warm a given material by one degree. Water has an unusually *high* specific heat: it soaks up lots of energy for only a small temperature rise, so it heats and cools slowly. Sand has a low specific heat and so heats up fast — hence the hot sand, cool sea. This is also why water is great for storing warmth.

**Thermal expansion** is the tendency of materials to get slightly bigger when heated — their molecules jiggle harder and need more room. Bridges include gaps and railway tracks small spaces so they do not buckle in summer heat.

**Phase changes** are switches between solid, liquid, and gas (for example melting or boiling). Here is the surprising part: while a substance is melting or boiling, its **temperature stays constant** even as you keep adding heat. That energy, called **latent heat**, goes into breaking the bonds holding molecules together rather than speeding them up. So boiling water holds at 100 C — extra heat turns liquid into steam instead of raising the temperature. (To freeze or condense, that same energy is released back out.)

**Recap.** High specific heat means slow to change temperature (water). Heating usually causes expansion. During melting and boiling, added latent heat changes the phase while the temperature holds steady.

items.prompt: At the beach the sand gets scorching while the sea stays cool under the same sun. The main reason is that water has a:
choices: Low specific heat, so it warms quickly
High specific heat, so it needs lots of energy for even a small temperature rise
Special ability to reflect all sunlight
Higher temperature than sand to begin with
items.explanation: Water's high specific heat means it absorbs a great deal of energy for a small temperature change, so it warms slowly while low-specific-heat sand heats fast. Choice 1 has it backwards; the effect is about heat capacity, not reflection (choice 3) or a head start in temperature (choice 4).

items.prompt: While a pot of water is boiling steadily, you keep adding heat. What happens to the water's temperature?
choices: It keeps climbing well above 100 C
It stays constant at about 100 C while the added heat turns liquid into steam
It drops because boiling cools the water
It rises and falls randomly
items.explanation: During boiling the temperature holds steady because the added energy (latent heat) breaks molecular bonds to make steam rather than raising the temperature. It does not climb past the boiling point while liquid remains (choice 1), boiling does not cool it (choice 3), and the temperature is steady, not erratic (choice 4).

items.prompt: 'Latent heat' is the energy that, during a phase change, goes into:
choices: Raising the temperature as fast as possible
Breaking (or forming) the bonds between molecules, with no change in temperature
Destroying molecules permanently
Cooling the surroundings instantly
items.explanation: Latent heat is absorbed or released to change the phase — melting, boiling, freezing, condensing — while the temperature stays put. It is precisely *not* raising temperature (choice 1); molecules are rearranged, not destroyed (choice 3); and it does not instantly cool surroundings (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why do engineers leave small gaps in bridges and between railway track sections?
choices: To save on building materials
To allow for thermal expansion so the structure does not buckle when it heats up
Because the metal shrinks in summer heat
To let rainwater drain through
items.explanation: Heated materials expand, so gaps give them room to grow without buckling. The gaps are about expansion, not saving material (choice 1); metal expands rather than shrinks when heated (choice 3); and drainage is not the purpose (choice 4).

items.prompt: On a heating curve (temperature plotted against heat added), the flat plateaus represent:
choices: Moments when no heat is being added
Phase changes, where heat goes into melting or boiling while the temperature stays constant
Times when the substance is cooling down
Errors in the measurement
items.explanation: Flat sections mark melting and boiling: heat keeps flowing in, but the temperature holds steady because the energy changes the phase. Heat is still being added on the plateau (choice 1); the substance is being heated, not cooled (choice 3); and the flat parts are real physics, not errors (choice 4).

items.prompt: Most materials expand slightly when heated because their molecules:
choices: Jiggle harder and need a little more room
Multiply into more molecules
Slow down and clump tightly together
Turn into heat itself
items.explanation: Added heat makes molecules vibrate more vigorously, pushing them slightly farther apart, so the material expands. Molecules are not created (choice 2); heating speeds them up rather than slowing them (choice 3); and molecules do not become heat (choice 4).

items.prompt: A material with a HIGH specific heat will, for the same amount of heat added:
choices: Change temperature by a large amount very quickly
Change temperature only a little, resisting fast heating or cooling
Refuse to absorb any heat at all
Always stay at absolute zero
items.explanation: High specific heat means a lot of energy is needed per degree, so the temperature changes slowly — like water. A large, fast change (choice 1) describes a low specific heat; it does absorb heat (choice 2 is correct, so choice 3 is wrong); and absolute zero is unrelated (choice 4).

items.prompt: When water freezes into ice, what happens to the latent heat involved?
choices: Latent heat must be added to freeze the water
Latent heat is released (given out) as the molecules lock into a solid
No energy is involved in freezing at all
The water's temperature rises sharply as it freezes
items.explanation: Freezing is the reverse of melting, so the latent heat absorbed during melting is released when the liquid locks into a solid. Energy is given out, not added (choice 1); freezing definitely involves energy (choice 3); and the temperature stays at the freezing point during the change rather than spiking (choice 4).

lessons.title: The Laws of Thermodynamics (Plain Language)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why can a hot drink cool down on its own but never spontaneously reheat? Why does a tidy room slowly become a mess but never tidy itself? And why does a bike pump get warm when you squeeze the air? These everyday facts are governed by the **laws of thermodynamics** — the rules about heat and energy. ('Thermodynamics' simply means heat-in-motion.)

**Build.** The **First Law** is *energy is conserved*: energy can be moved or changed from one form to another, but it is never created or destroyed. Heat is just one form of energy, so the total always balances — like money moving between pockets, never vanishing. A toaster turns electrical energy into heat; nothing is lost overall.

The **Second Law** has two everyday faces. First, *heat flows on its own only from hot to cold*, never the reverse — which is why drinks cool but do not self-heat. Second, *entropy tends to increase*. **Entropy** is a measure of disorder or 'mixed-up-ness.' Left alone, systems drift from order toward disorder: rooms mess up, smells spread, ice melts into a puddle. Reversing that (tidying, cooling a fridge) is possible only by spending energy from outside.

Finally, a gentle **gas law** idea: squeeze a gas into a smaller space and its molecules hit the walls more often, so the **pressure** (the push of the gas on its container) goes up. Squeezing also warms it — that is the warm bike pump. Heating a gas raises its pressure too.

**Recap.** First Law: energy is conserved. Second Law: heat flows hot-to-cold and disorder (entropy) tends to grow. Squeeze a gas and its pressure rises.

items.prompt: The First Law of Thermodynamics says that energy:
choices: Can be created whenever we need more of it
Is conserved — it can change form or move, but is never created or destroyed
Always disappears a little each time it is used
Only exists as heat and nothing else
items.explanation: The First Law is conservation of energy: the total stays fixed, though energy can change form. It cannot be created on demand (choice 1) or truly destroyed (choice 3 — it spreads out but is not lost), and heat is only one of many forms of energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: A hot cup of tea always cools toward room temperature and never reheats itself. Which law explains this?
choices: The First Law, because energy is created in the cup
The Second Law, because heat flows on its own only from hot to cold
No law; it is simply random chance
The First Law, because heat is destroyed as it cools
items.explanation: The Second Law states heat spontaneously moves from hotter to cooler, so tea loses heat to the room and cannot gather it back unaided. Energy is not created (choice 1) or destroyed (choice 4), and the one-way direction is a firm law, not chance (choice 3).

items.prompt: In thermodynamics, 'entropy' is a measure of:
choices: The temperature of an object
The disorder or 'mixed-up-ness' of a system
The total energy stored in an object
The speed of a single molecule
items.explanation: Entropy measures disorder; the Second Law says it tends to increase over time. It is not temperature (choice 1), not total stored energy (choice 3), and not the speed of one molecule (choice 4) — those are different quantities.

items.prompt: Which everyday observation best illustrates entropy tending to increase?
choices: A bedroom gradually getting messier unless you actively tidy it
A ball rolling at constant speed on smooth ice
A magnet sticking to a fridge door
A mirror reflecting your image
items.explanation: Systems left alone drift from order to disorder, so a room messes up on its own and only tidies when you spend energy. Constant rolling (choice 2), magnetism (choice 3), and reflection (choice 4) do not capture the spontaneous rise in disorder.

items.prompt: You squeeze the air in a sealed bike pump into a smaller volume (without letting heat escape). What happens to the pressure?
choices: It goes down because there is less air
It goes up because the molecules hit the walls more often in the smaller space
It stays exactly the same
It disappears entirely
items.explanation: Compressing the gas crowds the same molecules into less room, so they strike the walls more frequently and pressure rises. There is the same amount of air, just less space (choice 1); the pressure clearly changes (choices 3 and 4 are wrong).

items.prompt: A bike pump feels warm after you pump it hard. Does this break the First Law (energy conservation)?
choices: Yes, because new heat energy was created from nothing
No, because the work your muscles did was converted into heat — energy just changed form
Yes, because pumping destroys some energy
No, because heat is not a form of energy
items.explanation: Your muscular effort (work) is converted into heat in the gas, so energy merely changes form and the First Law holds. No energy is created (choice 1) or destroyed (choice 3), and heat is indeed a form of energy (choice 4).

items.prompt: In a gas, 'pressure' is best described as:
choices: The temperature of the gas
The push the gas exerts on the walls of its container as molecules strike them
The total number of molecules present
The color of the gas
items.explanation: Pressure is the force per area the gas applies to its container as countless molecules collide with the walls. Temperature (choice 1) and molecule count (choice 3) are related but distinct quantities, and color (choice 4) is irrelevant to pressure.

items.prompt: Can you cool the inside of a refrigerator, lowering its entropy, without violating the Second Law?
choices: No, because lowering entropy anywhere is impossible
Yes, by spending energy that increases disorder elsewhere (the fridge dumps heat into the room)
Yes, and it requires no energy at all
No, because heat naturally flows from cold to hot
items.explanation: You can lower entropy in one spot by spending energy, which raises entropy more elsewhere — the fridge pumps heat out into the room. Local order is allowed (choice 1 is wrong), it definitely needs energy (choice 3), and heat does not flow cold-to-hot on its own (choice 4).

lessons.title: Electric Charge
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Rub a balloon on your hair and it sticks to a wall; pull off a wool sweater in the dark and you hear tiny crackles. Both are the same invisible thing at work: **electric charge**.

**Build.** All stuff is made of tiny particles called **atoms**. Atoms contain even smaller bits that carry **charge** — a basic property that makes them push or pull on each other. There are two kinds, named **positive (+)** and **negative (−)**. The simple rule: **like charges repel** (two positives, or two negatives, push apart) and **opposite charges attract** (a positive and a negative pull together).

Normally an object has equal positive and negative charge, so it is **neutral** (no net push or pull). When you rub two things together, some negative bits (called **electrons** — the lightweight negative particles) rub off one onto the other. Now one object has extra negative charge and the other has extra positive. This stored, non-moving charge is **static electricity**, and the attraction it makes is why the balloon sticks.

Materials differ in how easily charge moves through them. A **conductor** (like metal) lets charge flow freely. An **insulator** (like rubber or plastic) holds charge in place. Around any charge is an **electric field** — an invisible region where another charge would feel a push or pull. We draw it with arrows pointing away from + and toward −.

**Recap.** Charge comes in + and −; like repels, opposite attracts. Rubbing moves electrons to make static charge. Conductors let charge flow, insulators don’t, and an electric field is the reach of a charge’s push.

items.prompt: What happens when you bring two negatively charged objects close together?
choices: They repel (push apart), because like charges repel
They attract, because all charges attract
Nothing, because only positives can interact
They become neutral instantly
items.explanation: Two negatives are 'like' charges, so they push apart. Choice 2 forgets that opposite charges attract but like charges repel; choice 3 wrongly claims negatives do nothing; choice 4 invents instant neutralizing that doesn't happen just from being near.

items.prompt: A positive charge and a negative charge are placed near each other. What do they do?
choices: Attract — opposite charges pull together
Repel — they always push apart
Ignore each other entirely
Spin in circles forever with no net force
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract, so + and − pull toward each other. Choice 2 confuses the rule for like charges; choices 3 and 4 deny the real attractive force between them.

items.prompt: When you rub a balloon on your hair and it picks up negative charge, what actually moved?
choices: Electrons (tiny negative particles) rubbed from your hair onto the balloon
Positive particles flew off the balloon into the air
Whole atoms jumped from hair to balloon
Nothing moved; the balloon just got warmer
items.explanation: Rubbing transfers lightweight electrons; gaining extra electrons makes the balloon negative. Choice 2 misnames what moves — it's electrons, not positives leaving; choice 3 overstates it (only electrons move, not whole atoms); choice 4 ignores the charge transfer.

items.prompt: An object is described as 'neutral'. What does that mean?
choices: It has equal amounts of positive and negative charge, so no net charge
It has no atoms in it at all
It is made only of positive charge
It cannot ever become charged
items.explanation: Neutral means the positive and negative charges balance out to zero net charge. Choice 2 is impossible for ordinary matter; choice 3 describes a charged object; choice 4 is wrong because rubbing can charge a neutral object.

items.prompt: Which material is the best example of a conductor?
choices: A copper wire
A rubber band
A glass cup
A dry wooden stick
items.explanation: Metals like copper let charge flow freely, so copper is a conductor. Rubber, glass, and dry wood are insulators that hold charge in place rather than letting it move.

items.prompt: What is an 'electric field' around a charge?
choices: A region of space where another charge would feel a push or pull
A wire that carries charge between objects
The total number of electrons in an object
A metal box that blocks all charge
items.explanation: A field is the invisible reach of a charge — anywhere a second charge would feel force. Choice 2 describes a conductor, not a field; choice 3 confuses field with a count of charge; choice 4 describes shielding, not the field itself.

items.prompt: In a field diagram, electric field arrows point away from a positive charge and toward a negative charge. What do these arrows represent?
choices: The direction a small positive test charge would be pushed
The path electrons take inside a metal wire
The speed of light leaving the charge
The temperature of the charged object
items.explanation: By convention field arrows show the force direction on a positive test charge: away from + and toward −. Choice 2 is about current in wires, not fields; choices 3 and 4 are unrelated quantities.

items.prompt: You walk across a carpet and get a shock touching a doorknob. Why?
choices: You built up static charge, which jumped to the metal knob as a spark
The doorknob is a battery storing voltage
Your body generated new atoms that escaped
Heat from your hand ignited the air
items.explanation: Friction with the carpet transfers electrons, leaving you charged; the extra charge leaps to the conducting knob as a spark. Choice 2 wrongly treats the knob as a power source; choice 3 invents new matter; choice 4 misattributes it to heat.

items.prompt: Why does a neutral wall attract a charged balloon?
choices: The balloon's charge nudges the wall's charges, pulling opposite ones slightly closer (induced attraction)
The wall is also strongly charged the same way
Gravity from the balloon pulls the wall
Air pressure glues them together
items.explanation: A charged balloon rearranges the wall's own charges so the opposite ones sit nearer the balloon, creating a net attraction even though the wall is neutral overall. Choice 2 is wrong (the wall stays neutral); choices 3 and 4 misname the cause.

lessons.title: Current, Voltage, Resistance & Ohm's Law
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a switch and a light comes on. Behind that simple act are three ideas that, once you picture them as water in pipes, become easy: **current**, **voltage**, and **resistance**.

**Build.** Imagine water flowing in a loop of pipes pushed by a pump.

**Current** is the *flow* — how much charge passes a point each second. It is measured in **amperes (amps, A)**. In a wire, current is moving charge, just as flow is moving water.

**Voltage** is the *push* — like the pressure the pump provides. More pressure shoves more water; more voltage pushes more charge. Voltage is measured in **volts (V)**. A battery is like a pump that supplies this push.

**Resistance** is how much the path *fights* the flow — like a narrow section of pipe. A narrow pipe lets less water through for the same pressure. Resistance is measured in **ohms (Ω, the Greek letter omega)**.

These three connect through **Ohm’s law**: $$V = I\\,R$$ — voltage equals current times resistance ($I$ is the symbol for current, $R$ for resistance). In words: for a fixed resistance, more voltage means more current; for a fixed voltage, more resistance means less current. Rearranged, $I = V/R$.

A common myth: that a bulb 'uses up' the current. It does not — the same amount of charge that flows in flows out. What the bulb uses up is **energy**, which the charge carries and the bulb turns into light and heat.

**Recap.** Voltage is the push (volts), current is the flow (amps), resistance is the squeeze (ohms), tied together by $V = I\\,R$. Bulbs use energy, not current.

items.prompt: In the water-pipe analogy, what does VOLTAGE correspond to?
choices: The water pressure pushing the flow
The amount of water flowing per second
How narrow the pipe is
The temperature of the water
items.explanation: Voltage is the push, just like pressure from a pump. Choice 2 is current (the flow rate); choice 3 is resistance (the narrowing); choice 4 has no role in the basic analogy.

items.prompt: What does electric CURRENT measure, and in what unit?
choices: How much charge flows past a point each second, in amperes (amps)
The push behind the charge, in volts
How hard the path fights flow, in ohms
The energy stored in a battery, in joules
items.explanation: Current is the rate of charge flow, measured in amps. Choice 2 describes voltage; choice 3 describes resistance; choice 4 describes stored energy, not current.

items.prompt: Resistance is to a circuit as ___ is to the water analogy.
choices: a narrow section of pipe that restricts flow
the pump that drives the water
the total volume of water in the loop
the color of the pipe
items.explanation: Resistance fights the flow like a narrow pipe restricts water. The pump is voltage (the push), total water relates to charge, and pipe color is irrelevant.

items.prompt: Ohm's law is written V = I R. What does it say in plain words?
choices: Voltage equals current times resistance
Voltage equals current divided by resistance
Current equals voltage times resistance
Resistance equals voltage times current
items.explanation: V = I R means voltage = current × resistance. Choice 2 wrongly divides; choice 3 rearranges incorrectly (it should be I = V/R); choice 4 confuses resistance with power (P = V I).

items.prompt: A 12-volt battery drives a current through a 4-ohm resistor. Using I = V/R, what is the current?
choices: 3 amps
48 amps
0.33 amps
16 amps
items.explanation: I = V/R = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 amps. Choice 2 multiplies instead of dividing (12×4); choice 3 inverts it (4÷12); choice 4 adds the numbers (12+4).

items.prompt: For a fixed resistance, what happens to the current if you double the voltage?
choices: The current doubles too
The current is halved
The current stays the same
The current drops to zero
items.explanation: Since I = V/R, doubling V (with R fixed) doubles I. Choice 2 reverses the relationship; choice 3 ignores Ohm's law; choice 4 is simply wrong.

items.prompt: A common misconception is that a light bulb 'uses up the current.' What is actually true?
choices: The current flowing out of the bulb equals the current flowing in; the bulb uses up ENERGY, not current
Half the current disappears inside the bulb
The bulb destroys the electrons that reach it
Current only flows on the way to the bulb, never back
items.explanation: Charge is conserved: the same current that enters the bulb leaves it. What the bulb consumes is energy, converted to light and heat. Choices 2 and 3 wrongly destroy charge; choice 4 forgets a circuit is a complete loop.

items.prompt: Why must a circuit form a complete loop for current to flow?
choices: Charge needs a continuous path back to the source, like water needs a closed loop to keep circulating
Because electrons are afraid of dead ends
Only complete loops can store voltage
A loop makes the wire colder
items.explanation: Charge flows only when there is an unbroken path from the source through the components and back; break the loop and flow stops, just like cutting a water loop stops circulation. The other choices are nonsense distractors.

items.prompt: Which set correctly pairs each quantity with its unit?
choices: Voltage – volts, Current – amps, Resistance – ohms
Voltage – amps, Current – ohms, Resistance – volts
Voltage – ohms, Current – volts, Resistance – amps
Voltage – watts, Current – volts, Resistance – amps
items.explanation: Voltage is in volts, current in amps, resistance in ohms. The other options scramble the units; watts (choice 4) measure power, not voltage.

items.prompt: If you keep the voltage the same but swap in a resistor with MORE resistance, the current will:
choices: Decrease, because a higher resistance fights the flow more (I = V/R)
Increase, because resistance helps push charge
Stay exactly the same
Become negative
items.explanation: With V fixed, raising R lowers I (I = V/R) — a narrower pipe lets less through. Choice 2 misunderstands resistance as a push; choice 3 ignores Ohm's law; choice 4 is impossible here.

lessons.title: Simple Circuits: Series, Parallel & Power
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Old holiday lights where one dead bulb killed the whole string — versus your home, where unplugging a lamp doesn’t shut off the fridge. The difference is **series** versus **parallel** wiring.

**Build.** A **series** circuit connects components one after another in a single loop, like beads on one string. The **same current** flows through every part, but the **voltage splits** among them. The catch: break any one part and the whole loop dies — that’s the old light string.

A **parallel** circuit gives each component its own branch off the main wires, like rungs of a ladder. Here **each branch gets the full voltage** of the source, and the current divides among the branches. Break one branch and the others keep working. This is why **home wiring is parallel**: every outlet receives the full voltage, and devices run independently.

Electricity does work, and the rate of doing it is **power** — energy used per second, measured in **watts (W)**. For circuits, power is voltage times current: $$P = V\\,I$$ — a higher push or a bigger flow means more power. A 100-watt bulb uses energy twice as fast as a 50-watt one.

Your electric bill, though, charges for total **energy**, not power. Energy = power × time. Run 1000 watts for 1 hour and you use one **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** — the standard billing unit. That’s why bills are in kWh: they measure how much energy you consumed over time, not the instantaneous rate.

**Recap.** Series shares current and splits voltage (one break stops all); parallel gives each branch full voltage and works independently (so homes use it). Power $P = V\\,I$ is energy per second in watts; bills use kWh, which is energy.

items.prompt: In a SERIES circuit, what is true about the current?
choices: The same current flows through every component
Each component gets its own separate current
Current only flows through the first component
There is no current in a series circuit
items.explanation: A series circuit is one single loop, so the same current passes through every part. Choice 2 describes parallel branches; choices 3 and 4 deny that the whole loop carries current.

items.prompt: Why does home wiring use PARALLEL connections?
choices: So each outlet gets the full voltage and devices work independently
So all devices must be on at the same time
Because parallel uses no electricity
So one broken bulb shuts off the whole house
items.explanation: In parallel each branch receives the full source voltage and one device failing doesn't cut the others. Choice 2 and 4 actually describe series behavior; choice 3 is false (parallel still uses energy).

items.prompt: Two identical bulbs are wired in PARALLEL across a battery. How much voltage does each bulb get?
choices: The full battery voltage — each branch sees the whole voltage
Half the battery voltage each
Zero, because they share
Double the battery voltage
items.explanation: In parallel every branch is connected directly across the source, so each bulb gets the full voltage. Choice 2 describes splitting in series; choices 3 and 4 are impossible.

items.prompt: In a string of bulbs wired in series, one bulb burns out. What happens?
choices: All the bulbs go dark, because the single loop is broken
Only the broken bulb goes dark; the rest stay lit
The other bulbs get brighter
Nothing changes at all
items.explanation: A series circuit is one loop; breaking it anywhere stops the whole current, so all bulbs go out. Choice 2 describes parallel behavior; choices 3 and 4 contradict the broken loop.

items.prompt: Electrical power is given by P = V I. What does power measure?
choices: The rate at which energy is used (energy per second), in watts
The total energy stored, in joules
The push of the source, in volts
The resistance of the wire, in ohms
items.explanation: Power is energy per second, measured in watts, and equals voltage times current. Choice 2 is total energy (not the rate); choice 3 is voltage; choice 4 is resistance.

items.prompt: A device runs at 120 volts and draws 2 amps. Using P = V I, what is its power?
choices: 240 watts
60 watts
122 watts
2 watts
items.explanation: P = V × I = 120 × 2 = 240 watts. Choice 2 divides (120÷2); choice 3 adds (120+2); choice 4 just repeats the current.

items.prompt: Why are electricity bills measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh)?
choices: A kWh measures total energy used (power × time), which is what you actually consume
Because a kWh measures voltage
Because a kWh is the same as a watt
Because bills only care about the instant a device is on
items.explanation: A kWh is energy: 1000 watts running for 1 hour. Bills charge for energy over time, not the instantaneous power. Choice 2 confuses energy with voltage; choice 3 is wrong (a kWh is energy, a watt is power); choice 4 ignores the role of time.

items.prompt: How much energy does a 1000-watt heater use if it runs for 1 hour?
choices: 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh)
1000 kilowatt-hours
1 watt
60 kilowatt-hours
items.explanation: 1000 watts = 1 kilowatt; running for 1 hour gives 1 kW × 1 h = 1 kWh. Choice 2 forgets to convert watts to kilowatts; choice 3 confuses energy with power; choice 4 wrongly multiplies by minutes.

items.prompt: In a SERIES circuit with two equal resistors, what happens to the source voltage?
choices: It splits, so each resistor gets a share of the total voltage
Each resistor gets the full source voltage
The voltage disappears entirely
The voltage doubles across each resistor
items.explanation: In series the same current flows but the voltage is divided among components. Choice 2 describes parallel; choices 3 and 4 are impossible.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation matches a PARALLEL circuit?
choices: Unplugging your toaster does not turn off the kitchen lights
Old holiday lights where one dead bulb kills the whole string
A flashlight that only works if every battery is present in one line
A single wire loop with one switch and one bulb
items.explanation: Parallel branches work independently, so removing one device leaves the others on — just like home wiring. Choices 2 and 3 describe series circuits (one break stops everything); choice 4 is a simple single loop.

lessons.title: Magnetism & Electromagnetism
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A fridge magnet, a compass that always finds north, and the motor spinning inside a fan are all the same physics — and, astonishingly, magnetism and electricity are two sides of one thing.

**Build.** A **magnet** is an object that pulls on certain metals (like iron). Every magnet has two ends called **poles**: a **north (N)** pole and a **south (S)** pole. The rule echoes charges: **like poles repel, opposite poles attract**. Around a magnet is a **magnetic field** — the region where its force acts — drawn as lines looping from N to S.

A key fact: you can never get a lone pole. Cut a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller magnets, each with its own N and S. There are no **magnetic monopoles** (single isolated poles) — unlike electric charge, which can be purely + or purely −.

The Earth itself is a giant magnet, so a **compass** (a tiny pivoting magnet) lines up with Earth’s magnetic field and points roughly north.

Now the deep link. **Electric current makes magnetism:** send current through a coil of wire and it behaves like a magnet — an **electromagnet**. Turn the current off and the magnetism vanishes, which is how junkyard cranes grab and drop cars. And the reverse holds: **a changing magnetic field makes current.** Spin a magnet near a coil and you push current through it.

These two effects power our world. A **motor** uses current in a magnetic field to make motion (fans, drills). A **generator** runs it backward — motion plus magnetism makes current (power plants).

**Recap.** Magnets have N and S poles (like repel, opposite attract); poles can’t be isolated. Current makes magnetism (electromagnets, motors) and changing magnetism makes current (generators).

items.prompt: What happens when you bring the NORTH pole of one magnet near the NORTH pole of another?
choices: They repel, because like poles repel
They attract, because all poles attract
Nothing happens between poles
One pole turns into a south pole
items.explanation: Like poles (N–N or S–S) push apart, mirroring like charges. Choice 2 forgets that only opposite poles attract; choice 3 denies the force; choice 4 invents a pole flip that doesn't occur.

items.prompt: You cut a bar magnet exactly in half. What do you get?
choices: Two smaller magnets, each with its own north and south pole
One piece that is only north and one that is only south
Two pieces with no magnetism at all
A single isolated north pole
items.explanation: Magnetic poles always come in pairs, so cutting a magnet yields two complete magnets. Choices 2 and 4 describe isolated poles (monopoles), which don't exist; choice 3 wrongly removes all magnetism.

items.prompt: Why can't you isolate a single magnetic pole (a monopole)?
choices: Magnetic poles always occur in N–S pairs; no isolated pole has ever been found
Because poles are made of electric charge
Because magnets are too small to cut
You can — every broken magnet gives one lone pole
items.explanation: Unlike electric charge (which can be purely + or −), magnetic poles always come paired N and S, so no monopole exists. Choice 2 conflates poles with charge; choice 3 is irrelevant; choice 4 directly contradicts the rule.

items.prompt: Why does a compass needle point roughly north?
choices: The Earth acts like a giant magnet, and the needle (a small magnet) aligns with Earth's magnetic field
The needle is attracted to the cold of the North Pole
Gravity pulls the needle northward
The Sun magnetizes the needle each morning
items.explanation: Earth's own magnetic field steers the free-pivoting compass magnet so it lines up north–south. Choice 2 confuses temperature with magnetism; choice 3 misattributes it to gravity; choice 4 is fictional.

items.prompt: What is an electromagnet?
choices: A coil of wire that becomes magnetic when electric current flows through it
A permanent magnet that never loses its magnetism
A magnet made by rubbing two iron bars
A type of battery that stores magnetism
items.explanation: Running current through a coil creates magnetism — an electromagnet — which switches off when the current stops. Choice 2 describes a permanent magnet; choice 3 is a different (weak) method; choice 4 misdescribes it as a battery.

items.prompt: A junkyard crane uses an electromagnet to lift cars and then drop them. How does it release the car?
choices: It switches off the current, so the magnetism disappears and the car drops
It reverses gravity under the car
It heats the car until it falls
It cuts the car in half
items.explanation: An electromagnet is magnetic only while current flows; cutting the current removes the magnetism and releases the load. The other choices are physically impossible or irrelevant.

items.prompt: Which statement captures the two-way link between electricity and magnetism?
choices: Electric current creates magnetism, and a changing magnetic field creates electric current
Electricity and magnetism are completely unrelated
Only magnets can make electricity; current never makes magnetism
Magnetism only exists inside batteries
items.explanation: This two-way relationship (current makes magnetism; changing magnetism makes current) underlies motors and generators. Choice 2 denies the link; choice 3 ignores half of it; choice 4 is false.

items.prompt: What is the basic idea behind an electric MOTOR?
choices: Current flowing in a magnetic field produces a force that creates motion
Motion alone with no electricity creates a spinning magnet
It turns light directly into heat
It stores charge like a battery
items.explanation: A motor uses current within a magnetic field to push parts and spin — turning electrical energy into motion. Choice 2 describes a generator's input, not a motor; choices 3 and 4 describe unrelated devices.

items.prompt: How does a GENERATOR at a power plant produce electricity?
choices: Motion spins magnets and coils relative to each other, and the changing magnetic field drives a current
It creates electrons out of nothing
It stores sunlight and releases it as current at night
It uses a battery to push current into the grid
items.explanation: A generator is a motor run backward: mechanical motion plus a changing magnetic field induces current. Choice 2 violates conservation of charge; choice 3 describes a different (solar) idea; choice 4 misdescribes a generator as a battery.

items.prompt: How does a magnetic field compare to an electric field in how we draw it?
choices: Magnetic field lines loop from a magnet's north pole around to its south pole
Magnetic field lines start and stop at a single isolated pole
Magnetic fields have no direction at all
Magnetic field lines always point straight up
items.explanation: We draw magnetic field lines running from N to S outside the magnet, forming closed loops — consistent with there being no isolated poles. Choice 2 implies a monopole; choices 3 and 4 misstate that fields have direction set by the poles.

lessons.title: What Light Is
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a switch and a room fills with light instantly. But what *is* light? It is the same kind of thing as a radio signal or an X-ray, just a different flavor. Understanding it explains rainbows, cameras, and why you can see at all.

**Build.** Light is an **electromagnetic wave** — a ripple of electric and magnetic energy that travels through empty space, needing no air or water to carry it. Like a wave on a pond, it has a **wavelength** (the distance from one crest to the next). Light's wavelength is tiny, far smaller than a hair.

When we study how light travels and bounces, we often draw it as a straight arrow called a **ray**. This is the **ray model**: light travels in **straight lines** until it hits something. That is why a flashlight makes a straight beam and why shadows have sharp edges.

Light is also astonishingly fast: about **300,000 kilometers per second** in a vacuum — around the Earth seven times in one second. We write its speed as $c$ (just a label for that number).

All electromagnetic waves form the **electromagnetic (EM) spectrum**, ordered by wavelength: radio (longest), then microwave, infrared, then **visible light** (the only part our eyes detect), then ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays (shortest). Visible light is a tiny slice of the whole spectrum.

**Recap.** Light is a fast electromagnetic wave that travels in straight rays at about 300,000 km/s; the colors we see are just a narrow band of a much larger spectrum.

items.prompt: In physics, light is best described as what kind of thing?
choices: A stream of tiny hot particles like dust
An electromagnetic wave
A flow of air pushed by a source
A type of sound that is too high to hear
items.explanation: Light is an electromagnetic wave — a ripple of electric and magnetic energy. It is not air or sound (those need a material to travel through, and sound is a pressure wave in matter). Light can cross the empty vacuum of space, which is how sunlight reaches us.

items.prompt: Roughly how fast does light travel in a vacuum?
choices: About 300 kilometers per second
About 300,000 kilometers per second
About 1,000 kilometers per hour
It is instant — it has no speed
items.explanation: Light moves at about 300,000 km per second, fast enough to circle Earth roughly seven times in one second. It is incredibly fast but not infinite or instant — sunlight, for example, takes about eight minutes to reach us.

items.prompt: Why does a flashlight beam appear as a straight line and shadows have sharp edges?
choices: Light curves gently around objects
Light travels in straight lines until it hits something
Light is pulled downward by gravity
Light bounces randomly in all directions from the source
items.explanation: In the ray model, light travels in straight lines. An object blocks the straight rays behind it, leaving a sharp shadow. If light curved around objects easily, shadows would be blurry and beams would bend.

items.prompt: What is the 'ray model' of light useful for?
choices: Drawing light as straight arrows to track where it goes
Measuring the temperature of a light source
Proving light is made of sound
Explaining why light needs air to travel
items.explanation: The ray model represents light as straight arrows (rays), which makes it easy to trace how light reflects and bends. It is a simplified tool for geometry; it does not measure temperature, and light does not need air to travel.

items.prompt: Visible light is what portion of the full electromagnetic spectrum?
choices: The entire spectrum — there is nothing else
A tiny slice of a much larger spectrum
Exactly half of the spectrum
The longest-wavelength part of the spectrum
items.explanation: Visible light is only a small band of the EM spectrum, which also includes radio, microwave, infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. Our eyes happen to detect just that narrow slice; the rest is invisible to us.

items.prompt: Which list places electromagnetic waves in order from longest wavelength to shortest?
choices: X-rays, visible light, radio
Radio, visible light, X-rays
Visible light, radio, X-rays
Gamma rays, radio, microwave
items.explanation: Going from longest to shortest wavelength: radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma. So radio (long) → visible (middle) → X-rays (short) is correct. The reversed order puts the shortest first, which is wrong.

items.prompt: What does 'wavelength' mean for a light wave?
choices: How bright the light is
The distance from one wave crest to the next
How long the light has existed
The total length of the beam
items.explanation: Wavelength is the distance between repeating points of the wave, like crest to crest. It is not brightness (that is the amount of light energy) nor the length of the whole beam. Different wavelengths of visible light appear as different colors.

items.prompt: How can sunlight reach Earth across the empty vacuum of space?
choices: Air in space carries it to us
An electromagnetic wave needs no material to travel through
The Sun blows it toward us like wind
Gravity drags the light across the gap
items.explanation: Electromagnetic waves can travel through a vacuum, needing no air or other medium. That is a key difference from sound, which must travel through matter. There is essentially no air in space, yet light crosses it just fine.

items.prompt: Which everyday device uses a part of the EM spectrum that our eyes cannot see?
choices: A candle, which gives off visible light
A TV remote, which sends infrared signals
A green laser pointer
A red traffic light
items.explanation: A TV remote uses infrared, which is invisible to human eyes but still electromagnetic. The candle, green laser, and red light are all visible light. This shows the spectrum extends beyond what we can see.

lessons.title: Reflection & Mirrors
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Look in a mirror and a copy of you looks back. Catch sunlight on a watch and you can flick a bright dot across a wall. Both are **reflection** — light bouncing off a surface — and it follows one neat rule.

**Build.** When a ray of light hits a surface, it bounces off. To describe the angles, we first draw a **normal**: an imaginary line that is perpendicular (at 90 degrees) to the surface at the point of contact. The incoming ray is the **incident ray**, and the bounced ray is the **reflected ray**.

The **law of reflection** says: the **angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection** (both measured from the normal, not the surface). Think of a ball thrown at a smooth floor — it bounces away at the same slant it came in.

A flat (**plane**) mirror has a smooth surface, so all rays obey the rule together and you get a clear image. A rough surface scatters rays in all directions (**diffuse reflection**) — that is why paper looks matte, not mirror-like.

**Curved** mirrors bend the rule to useful effect. A **concave** mirror (caved inward, like the inside of a spoon) can gather rays to a point and magnify — used in makeup mirrors and telescopes. A **convex** mirror (bulging outward) spreads rays and shows a wide view — used in car side mirrors and shop security mirrors.

Crucially, you see an object because light from a source bounces off it and travels into your eyes. Your eyes do **not** emit light.

**Recap.** Light reflects so that the angle in equals the angle out, measured from the normal; smooth surfaces give clear images, and we see by catching reflected light.

items.prompt: The law of reflection states that:
choices: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection
Light always bounces straight back the way it came
The reflected ray is twice the angle of the incident ray
Light slows down when it reflects
items.explanation: Angle in equals angle out, both measured from the normal. Light only bounces straight back if it hits exactly along the normal; at a slant it leaves at the same slant on the other side. Reflection changes direction, not speed.

items.prompt: Why are you able to see a wooden table that is not itself glowing?
choices: Your eyes send out rays that feel the table
Light from a source reflects off the table into your eyes
The table emits its own light
Air around the table carries its image to you
items.explanation: We see non-glowing objects because light from a source (Sun, lamp) bounces off them and enters our eyes. Eyes detect light; they do not emit it. This is a very common misconception worth correcting: vision is receiving, not sending.

items.prompt: The angles in the law of reflection are measured from what reference line?
choices: The surface itself
The normal (the line perpendicular to the surface)
The edge of the mirror
The horizon
items.explanation: Angles are measured from the normal, the line at 90 degrees to the surface where the ray hits. Measuring from the surface instead would give different numbers, so always use the normal as the reference.

items.prompt: A ray strikes a flat mirror at 30 degrees from the normal. At what angle does it leave?
choices: 60 degrees from the normal
30 degrees from the normal
0 degrees (straight back)
90 degrees from the normal
items.explanation: By the law of reflection the outgoing angle equals the incoming angle: 30 degrees from the normal. It does not double to 60, and it only goes straight back if the incoming angle were 0.

items.prompt: Why does a sheet of paper look matte (not mirror-like), even though it reflects light?
choices: Paper absorbs all the light that hits it
Its rough surface scatters rays in many directions (diffuse reflection)
Paper does not obey the law of reflection
Paper only reflects invisible light
items.explanation: Each tiny patch of paper still obeys the law of reflection, but the surface is rough, so rays scatter every which way — called diffuse reflection. That scattering prevents a clear image. A smooth surface keeps the rays organized, giving a mirror image.

items.prompt: Which mirror is curved outward (bulging) and gives a wide, shrunken view, like a car's side mirror?
choices: Concave mirror
Convex mirror
Plane mirror
Two-way mirror
items.explanation: A convex mirror bulges outward and spreads rays apart, giving a wide field of view with smaller images — ideal for car and shop mirrors. A concave mirror caves inward and can magnify; a plane mirror is flat.

items.prompt: A makeup mirror that magnifies your face is which type?
choices: Convex (bulging outward)
Concave (caved inward, like a spoon's inside)
Perfectly flat (plane)
A rough, diffuse surface
items.explanation: A concave mirror caves inward and can gather rays to enlarge a nearby image, which is why makeup and shaving mirrors magnify. A convex mirror would shrink the view, and a flat mirror keeps the same size.

items.prompt: In a dark room with no light source at all, why can't you see objects in a mirror?
choices: Mirrors stop working in the dark
There is no light to reflect off the objects into your eyes
Your eyes need to warm up first
The mirror absorbs the darkness
items.explanation: Seeing requires light bouncing from objects into your eyes. With no source, there is no light to reflect, so nothing is visible. The mirror is fine; it simply has no light to redirect. This reinforces that vision depends on received light.

items.prompt: If a ray hits a mirror exactly along the normal (head-on, 0 degrees), where does it go?
choices: It reflects straight back along the same path
It slides sideways along the surface
It leaves at 90 degrees
It passes through the mirror
items.explanation: With an incidence angle of 0, the reflection angle is also 0, so the ray returns straight back along its incoming path. It cannot leave at 90 degrees (that would be along the surface), and a mirror reflects rather than transmits.

lessons.title: Refraction & Lenses
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Put a straw in a glass of water and it looks bent or broken at the surface. The straw is fine — the light is what bends. This bending, called **refraction**, is the secret behind eyeglasses, cameras, magnifiers, and rainbows.

**Build.** Light travels fastest in a vacuum (and nearly as fast in air) but **slows down** in materials like water or glass. When a ray crosses the boundary between two materials *at an angle*, the change in speed makes it **change direction** — that is refraction. Going into a slower material (air into glass), the ray bends **toward** the normal (the perpendicular line). Coming back out into a faster material, it bends away. Hit the surface straight-on and it slows but does not turn.

A **prism** (a triangular glass block) bends light, and because each color slows by a slightly different amount, white light fans out into a band of colors — red bends least, violet most. This splitting is called **dispersion**, and it is how rainbows form.

A **lens** is a shaped piece of glass or plastic that uses refraction on purpose. A **converging (convex)** lens is thicker in the middle and bends parallel rays inward to meet at a **focal point** — used in magnifiers, cameras, and the human eye. A **diverging (concave)** lens is thinner in the middle and spreads rays apart — used to correct nearsightedness.

**Recap.** Light slows in denser materials, and crossing a boundary at an angle bends it; lenses harness this bending to focus or spread light for glasses, cameras, and magnifiers.

items.prompt: What is refraction?
choices: Light bouncing off a shiny surface
Light changing direction as it passes from one material into another
Light being absorbed and turned into heat
Light splitting into a beam and a shadow
items.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light when it crosses from one material to another and changes speed. Bouncing off a surface is reflection, and being absorbed is something different. Refraction is why a submerged straw looks bent.

items.prompt: What actually causes the light to bend at a boundary?
choices: The light speeds up in the denser material
The light changes speed (slows in glass or water)
Gravity from the glass pulls on the light
The color of the material repels the light
items.explanation: Light slows down in denser materials like glass or water, and that change in speed at an angled boundary is what bends the ray. It does not speed up going into glass, and gravity is not the cause here.

items.prompt: When light passes from air into glass at an angle, it bends:
choices: Away from the normal
Toward the normal
Exactly 90 degrees every time
It never bends entering glass
items.explanation: Entering a slower medium (air to glass), the ray bends toward the normal. Going from glass back into air, it bends away from the normal. The amount depends on the angle, not a fixed 90 degrees.

items.prompt: Why does a straw in a glass of water appear bent at the water's surface?
choices: The water physically bends the straw
Light from the underwater part refracts as it leaves the water, so it reaches your eye at a different angle
The straw reflects light back on itself
Water magnifies the straw evenly
items.explanation: Light from the submerged part bends as it exits the water into air, so your eye traces it back to the wrong spot, making the straw look bent or shifted. The straw itself is perfectly straight; refraction fools your eye.

items.prompt: A prism splits white light into a band of colors because:
choices: The prism adds color to the white light
Each color slows and bends by a slightly different amount (dispersion)
White light is actually just one color
The glass paints the light with a rainbow
items.explanation: White light is already a mix of all colors. In glass each color bends by a slightly different amount (dispersion), so they fan apart — violet bends most, red least. The prism reveals the colors; it does not add them.

items.prompt: A converging (convex) lens does what to parallel rays of light?
choices: Spreads them apart
Brings them together to meet at a focal point
Reflects them backward
Leaves them unchanged
items.explanation: A convex lens (thicker in the middle) refracts parallel rays inward so they meet at the focal point. A diverging lens would spread rays apart instead. This focusing ability is what lets cameras and eyes form sharp images.

items.prompt: Which lens is thinner in the middle and spreads light rays apart?
choices: Converging (convex) lens
Diverging (concave) lens
Plane lens
Prism lens
items.explanation: A diverging (concave) lens is thinner in the middle and spreads rays outward; it is used to correct nearsightedness. A converging (convex) lens is thicker in the middle and focuses rays together.

items.prompt: If a ray of light hits a glass surface straight-on (along the normal), what happens?
choices: It bends sharply toward the normal
It slows down but does not change direction
It reflects straight back without entering
It splits into all the colors
items.explanation: Refraction only changes direction when light crosses a boundary at an angle. Hitting straight-on (along the normal), the light slows in the glass but keeps going straight. Bending requires an angled approach.

items.prompt: A simple magnifying glass makes small print look bigger because it is what kind of lens?
choices: A diverging (concave) lens that spreads rays
A converging (convex) lens that bends rays to enlarge the image
A flat sheet of glass
A mirror that reflects the print
items.explanation: A magnifier is a converging (convex) lens; it refracts light so the object appears larger. A diverging lens would make things look smaller, and flat glass would not magnify at all.

lessons.title: Color & Vision
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why is grass green and a banana yellow? And why do paints mix differently from stage lights? It all comes down to which wavelengths of light reach your eye.

**Build.** Color is how our eyes interpret the **wavelength** of visible light. Longer wavelengths look red; shorter ones look violet, with orange, yellow, green, and blue in between. White light (like sunlight) is all these wavelengths mixed together.

Objects have color because of what they do to light. A red apple looks red because it **reflects** red light and **absorbs** the rest. A green leaf reflects green and absorbs the others. A black object absorbs nearly all colors; a white object reflects nearly all. So the misconception that 'a red apple is full of red color' is backwards — it is rejecting red while soaking up everything else.

Mixing colors comes in two opposite kinds. **Additive mixing** combines *light*: starting from darkness, red, green, and blue (RGB) light add up to white. This is how TV and phone screens work. **Subtractive mixing** combines *pigments* (paint or ink): each pigment absorbs (subtracts) some wavelengths, so cyan, magenta, and yellow stacked together absorb everything and look black. Paints get darker as you mix; lights get brighter.

Your **eye** works like a camera: a convex lens focuses light onto the **retina** (a light-sensitive layer at the back), which sends signals to the brain. A camera does the same with a lens and a sensor.

**Recap.** Color is the wavelength your eye detects; objects show the colors they reflect while absorbing the rest, light mixes additively (toward white) and pigment subtractively (toward black), and the eye focuses images much like a camera.

items.prompt: What physical property of light does our sense of color correspond to?
choices: The brightness of the light
The wavelength of the light
The speed of the light
The temperature of the room
items.explanation: Color corresponds to wavelength: long wavelengths look red, short ones look violet. Brightness is the amount of light, and the speed of light is essentially the same for all colors in a vacuum. Wavelength is the key to color.

items.prompt: Why does a red apple look red in white light?
choices: It is filled with red light inside
It reflects red light and absorbs the other colors
It absorbs red and reflects everything else
It creates red light on its own
items.explanation: A red apple reflects red wavelengths to your eye while absorbing the rest. The common backwards idea is that it 'contains' or absorbs red — in fact it rejects (reflects) red and soaks up the others. Objects show the colors they reflect.

items.prompt: What does a black object do to most of the light that hits it?
choices: Reflects almost all of it
Absorbs almost all of it
Bends almost all of it
Splits it into colors
items.explanation: Black objects absorb nearly all visible wavelengths, reflecting little back, which is why they look dark and warm up in sunlight. A white object does the opposite, reflecting nearly all colors.

items.prompt: In additive mixing, combining red, green, and blue LIGHT produces:
choices: Black
White
Brown
Gray
items.explanation: Adding red, green, and blue light together gives white — this is how screens make white from RGB pixels. Mixing toward black happens with pigments (subtractive mixing), not with light.

items.prompt: When you mix many different paints together, the result tends to get darker because:
choices: Paint adds light like a screen does
Each pigment absorbs (subtracts) more wavelengths, leaving less light reflected
Paint reflects all colors equally
Darkness is a color you are adding
items.explanation: Pigments work by subtractive mixing: each one absorbs some wavelengths, so combining many leaves fewer wavelengths to reflect, trending toward black. This is the opposite of mixing light, which trends toward white.

items.prompt: A glowing phone screen makes its colors using which method?
choices: Subtractive mixing with cyan, magenta, yellow pigments
Additive mixing with red, green, blue light
Reflecting room light only
Bending sunlight through a prism
